finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and a police eyewitness, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of late 2025, the subject officer, operating a police cruiser with another officer as their passenger, stopped a vehicle in a rural area. The affected person was driving the vehicle. Police officers had been on the lookout for the affected person after they expressed suicidal ideations when they came to learn, earlier that day, that they were the subject of a sexual assault investigation. As the officers exited the cruiser to make their way to the vehicle, the affected person accessed a knife and used it to inflict a serious laceration across their neck. The subject officer and the other officer observed the wound, contacted paramedics, and attempted to apply first aid. When the affected person tried to prevent the officers from helping them, they were handcuffed to allow the officers to provide emergency care. The affected person was transported to hospital and treated for a serious neck wound.
A person died of a heart attack at their home after visiting the emergency room twice for chest pain. The coroner's office is recommending that the hospital review and improve its emergency care.

tribunal_body: PRC AB — ASIRT file 2024-0061(S) — Calgary Police Service — decision: https://albertaprc.ca/media/q5hdp1gv/asirt-dec-2024-0061-s-20251217.pdf

summary_redacted: In the evening of early 2026, police officers were on the lookout for a vehicle being operated by someone who was possibly impaired. Police received information that a suspected impaired driver had left an area. About ten minutes later, the subject officer radioed that they had located the vehicle and were pursuing it. The driver did not stop for the officer. Instead, they led the officer on a chase during which they disregarded traffic rules and travelled at high speeds. The driver lost control of the vehicle and collided with a utility pole at an intersection. The driver was transported to hospital and diagnosed with injuries. The subject officer was a few car lengths away from the driver at the time of the collision and had tracked the vehicle over a distance of about three kilometres, at times well over the speed limit and through traffic lights without stopping.

finding: unsubstantiated
summary_redacted: The Military Police Complaints Commission investigated an interference complaint filed by two Military Police members against a Warrant Officer and a superior officer in their chain of command. The complaint arose from a welfare check conducted at the residence of a Military Police officer whose young children were found unsupervised. Following the welfare check, a criminal investigation was opened for alleged child abandonment and failure to provide necessaries of life. The complainants alleged that the Warrant Officer improperly interfered by directing a 'tactical pause' and seeking to transfer the investigation file to the General Investigation Section, and that the superior officer improperly interfered by contacting a family member of the subject officer. The MPCC found all allegations of improper interference not substantiated, concluding the directions were reasonable and undertaken for legitimate purposes, and the contact did not interfere with the investigation. The investigation ultimately did not result in charges.
tribunal_body: MPCC file 2021-026 — Military Police Complaints Commission

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of late 2025, the affected person was on their way home from work travelling in Brampton. They had just taken the on-ramp to a highway when they were pulled over by a RIDE program set up on the ramp. The affected person was approached by an officer who asked them to pull ahead for further questioning. Another officer approached and asked the affected person to continue forward and stop their vehicle on the ramp shoulder in front of a police vehicle. They did so. The officer asked the affected person to step out of their vehicle and escorted them to the back of the vehicle. They were joined at this time by the subject officer. The affected person was angry about being pulled over. They would not allow the officer to fully read them a breath test demand and insisted the officer simply administer the test. The officer explained that they needed to read the demand in full and ensure they understood it. They told the affected person that refusing to take the test would constitute a criminal offence. The subject officer tried to calm the affected person but to no avail. When the affected person began to walk to the driver’s door of their vehicle, the officer grabbed and pushed them back towards the rear of the vehicle and a snow-covered area past the ramp shoulder. The affected person fell backwards over the ramp curb. They attempted to stand back up but were forced to the ground by the subject officer. There followed a struggle between the affected person and several police officers. The affected person was eventually handcuffed and placed in the rear seat of a police cruiser. The affected person attended hospital the next day and was diagnosed with a concussion.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, the affected person was at a public square to protest against a demonstration by a certain movement. The demonstrators had made their way to the public square from another location, where they had initially gathered. The affected person, with a group of other people, had also been at the initial location attempting to disrupt the demonstration. Police officers had been assigned to police the demonstration and counter-protest. They were to preserve the peace and attempt to keep the rival factions separated. At the public square, officers used their bicycles to erect a perimeter between the demonstrators, at the centre of the square, and the counter-protesters, on its periphery. The affected person was in the area of the northeast corner of the square when they and others were asked to leave the square. The affected person began to slowly walk away but stopped to object to the treatment of a woman, who was being physically escorted by police. The subject officer ordered them to leave or they would be arrested for obstructing police. The officer then pushed the affected person by the arms towards the roadway that bordered the square. The affected person told the officer that they did not have to touch them and turned to face the officer. The officer reacted by turning them around and pushing them in the back towards the roadway. The affected person stumbled forward and fell onto the sidewalk, fracturing their hip in the process. The affected person was transported by paramedics to hospital and treated for their injury.

A person died from septicemia caused by a liver and subdiaphragmatic abscess at their home. They had undergone an endoscopic intervention on the bile ducts a few weeks prior. The circumstances surrounding their death raise concerns about the care provided during their post-operative follow-up and the deployment of pre-hospital emergency services.

Deux pompiers volontaires se sont noyés lors d'une intervention d'urgence dans une ville au Québec au début 2023, alors qu'ils tentaient de secourir des résidents d'une maison entourée par une crue soudaine d'une rivière. L'enquête a révélé des lacunes importantes, notamment un plan d'urgence inadéquat, un manque de formation et d'équipement pour les interventions aquatiques, ainsi qu'une coordination insuffisante entre les ressources municipales.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In the morning of early 2026, members of the Emergency Response Team convened at a house situated in the area of Cornwall. Acting under the authority of a CDSA search warrant, the team entered the house to search for cocaine and fentanyl. The affected person was present at the time and alerted to the officers’ presence outside the home. They entered the main floor bathroom and attempted to conceal themselves behind a sink. They were quickly located by an officer and placed on the floor in a prone position. The subject officer, the team leader, had forced their way into the bathroom and located the affected person. Having positioned the affected person on the floor, the officer dropped their left knee intending to lower themselves by their side but inadvertently landed on the affected person’s back. The impact fractured the affected person’s left hand, which was underneath their chest at the time. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to the police station. They were seen at hospital later in the day and diagnosed with their injury.

summary_redacted_fr: Une plaignante a signalé à la police militaire des préoccupations concernant la sécurité d'un militaire. Elle allègue que la police militaire a rejeté ses préoccupations et retardé la vérification du bien-être, est restée à l'extérieur de la résidence pendant 45 minutes avant d'entrer, et a mal géré des éléments de preuve dont une note de suicide. Le militaire, retrouvé vivant par un service de police, est décédé à l'hôpital. Une commission a décidé de tenir une audience d'intérêt public en raison de la gravité des allégations, des implications systémiques concernant les vérifications du bien-être et la prévention du suicide au sein de la police militaire, et des obstacles à la divulgation opposés par un haut gradé.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire — CPPM-2024-037

Over 65 witnesses were interviewed and subject interviews were conducted in late 2018.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In the morning of early 2026, members of the Emergency Response Team convened at a house situated in the area of Cornwall. Acting under the authority of a search warrant, the team entered the house to search for cocaine and fentanyl. The affected person was present at the time and alerted to the officers’ presence outside the home. They entered the main floor bathroom and attempted to conceal themselves behind a sink. They were quickly located by an officer and placed on the floor in a prone position. The subject officer, the team leader, had forced their way into the bathroom and located the affected person. Having positioned the affected person on the floor, the officer dropped their left knee intending to lower themselves by their side but inadvertently landed on the affected person’s back. The impact fractured the affected person’s left hand, which was underneath their chest at the time. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to the police station. They were seen at hospital later in the day and diagnosed with their injury.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, the subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the evening of late 2025, police officers were dispatched to a location. The police had received a call about a disturbance involving the affected person and their partner. The subject officer arrived on scene and was joined by other officers. A crisis worker was also in attendance with the police. The subject officer was met by the affected person and allowed inside. The two made their way to the rear yard to talk. The other officers and the crisis worker remained in the location to speak with the partner. Over the course of the next 45 minutes, the subject officer attempted to have the affected person explain what had happened. The affected person engaged with the officer in fits and starts, unable to provide a coherent account of the events and occasionally adopting a belligerent posture. The officer told the affected person that they would not be allowed to stay at the location that night, and would need to explore alternative accommodations. The affected person became increasingly frustrated, unable to secure themselves a place for the night. Near the end of their discourse, the affected person walked towards the rear door of the location. The subject officer grabbed a hold of them, told them they were under arrest for public intoxication, and attempted to pull them away from the location. The affected person resisted the officer by pulling in the opposite direction. There followed an altercation. The subject officer threw the affected person to the ground and positioned themselves overtop their supine torso. The affected person thrashed their legs about. The subject officer delivered a series of elbow strikes to the affected person's face, after which their arms were controlled in front of their body and handcuffed. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fractured right shoulder.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officers chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2026, the subject officers, in the company of other officers, attended at an apartment in Oshawa. A neighbouring tenant had called 911 to report the sounds of a woman screaming coming from the apartment. Officers knocked on the door and asked the woman to open it. She tried but was unable to open the door. She could be heard moaning in distress and saying that she had been choked. An officer retrieved a ram and the subject officer used it to break open the door. The woman was by the doorway and pointed in the direction of a hallway. The affected person appeared in the hallway. He was at the apartment in violation of a bail order. An officer ordered the affected person to put his hands behind his back. The affected person refused to do so and instead walked towards the officer. The officer grabbed a hold of the affected person and attempted to wrestle control of his arms behind his back. The affected person refused to release his arms. There followed a struggle during which the affected person was forced to the hallway floor and subjected to multiple punches by the officers, and the use of a CEW, before he was handcuffed behind his back. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to hospital on two occasions before he was eventually diagnosed with a broken rib.
A decision was made in the matter of a shooting near Grande Prairie in early 2026.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and additional police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a location in response to a report of a person attempting to break into a residence. Two officers were the first on scene, arriving at about 3:10 a.m. They were joined shortly by a police dog handler, the subject officer, and their dog. Additional officers arrived on scene and set up a perimeter around the house as the subject officer deployed their dog to locate the suspect. It was not long before the dog led the subject officer to the rear yard and a crawl space underneath a deck. The affected person was hiding in the crawl space, which was cluttered with stored items. They failed to come out and give any indication of their presence despite repeated direction from the subject officer for them to do so. With the dog still attached to the lead, the subject officer released the dog into the crawl space. The dog’s first foray into the space was unsuccessful. The subject officer released the dog into the crawl space again. On this occasion, the dog located the affected person under some items and bit into their left arm. The affected person gave no reaction. The subject officer approached their dog and the affected person and released the dog’s hold, after which the affected person was taken into custody. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture of the left elbow and dog bites to the left arm.
A decision was made in the matter of a police shooting in Edmonton in early 2024.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a residence. A resident had called police to remove an intoxicated and unwelcome guest – the affected person – who was refusing to leave. Officers arrived on scene and took a belligerent affected person into custody to prevent a breach of the peace. They were placed in a cruiser and transported to the police detachment. The affected person remained confrontational at the detachment. At one point, after they had been stood facing a wall to be searched, the affected person turned and started to close their hand in the direction of one of the searching officers. The officer standing behind the affected person reacted by wrapping an arm around their neck, forcing them to the floor and delivering a punch in the direction of their upper body. The affected person, prone on the floor, did not readily release their arms at the officers’ direction and was met with several punches to the legs and torso by the officers. The officers wrestled control of their arms after the strikes and handcuffed them behind their back. The affected person was subsequently transported to hospital when they complained of facial pain. They were diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted_fr: À une date en 2021, un membre des Forces canadiennes a été détenu et menotté par des policiers militaires à la suite d'une vérification erronée dans le Centre d'information de la police canadienne (CIPC). Un mandat d'immigration a été confondu avec une personne sans vérification adéquate des identifiants. La CPPM a conclu que la détention était abusive, que le menottage était injustifié, et que la personne avait été victime de profilage racial. Des lacunes systémiques ont également été identifiées concernant la formation, la supervision et la documentation.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire (CPPM)

summary_redacted: In the afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer observed the affected person in a Stratford area. Aware that the affected person was subject to a warrant for their arrest, the officer contacted uniformed personnel to respond to the area to take the affected person into custody. A witness officer arrived in the area in a fully marked cruiser. The officer pulled up to the affected person as they walked and watched as the affected person reversed directions and ran. The subject officer stopped their vehicle, exited, and tackled the affected person to the ground. The affected person attempted to get up and was held down by the subject officer and the witness officer. A struggle ensued during which the subject officer delivered several punches to the head and torso of the affected person, and a knee strike to their left side. With the assistance of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a right orbital bone fracture.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person made their way to a bridge. Their intention was to jump from the bridge. They called 911 to advise emergency responders of their intention and provided them with their location. Two call-takers – one from a police service and another from the ambulance service – spoke to the affected person. They assured them they had done the right thing by calling them, asked them not to jump from the bridge, and told them help was on its way. The affected person stayed on the line for several minutes. At the very end, they told the police call-taker that they could hear and see responding police officers, and that they were going to jump. They proceeded to do just that. The subject officer was the first officer on scene. They stopped their cruiser in a parking lot by the bridge, exited and was making their way to the affected person’s position when the affected person jumped from the bridge onto some snow-covered rocks below. The officer provided care and comfort to the affected person while they waited for paramedics. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with fractures.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and other witnesses (police and non-police), gives rise to the following scenario. As was his legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU. He did authorize the release of his notes. Shortly before noon, in early 2026, police officers responded to a call in Sudbury. A 911 call had been received suggesting a potentially violent disturbance inside a vehicle. The caller did not communicate directly with the call-taker but the police were able to use her cell phone to approximate a location from where she was calling. An officer found the vehicle in the area. He spoke to two females and determined that there were grounds to believe that one of them had been assaulted by the complainant. Fearing his imminent arrest, the complainant, inside the rear of the vehicle, exited and fled the scene. He ran a short distance and entered into the rear, enclosed deck of a house in the area, where he sought to conceal himself in a crawl space under a staircase. A police dog handler and his dog arrived at the site of the vehicle from which the complainant had fled. Joined by other officers, the police dog handler and his dog initiated a track of the complainant. He was not hard to find. The officers followed a set of footprints in the snow to the house, spoke to one of the homeowners briefly, and entered the rear deck. The complainant was quickly located inside a crawl space. The police dog handler released his police dog. The dog approached the complainant and bit his right hand. With the dog still latched onto his hand, the complainant was removed from the crawl space and positioned on the floor in a prone position. He was handcuffed behind the back and the dog separated from his right hand. The complainant was transported to hospital after his arrest and diagnosed with a fracture of the small finger on the right hand.

A person died in a city due to a drug overdose. The death occurred after a recent hospitalization and medical leave. The coroner's office is recommending that the hospital revise its medical evaluation and follow-up procedures for patients with substance use disorders.

As part of a public interest investigation, the authorities examined allegations that a police unit's leadership improperly directed subordinate officers to pause a child welfare investigation and failed to lay Criminal Code charges (Failure to Provide Necessaries of Life, Child Abandonment) against an officer, potentially delaying intervention by child welfare authorities. The conduct of the unit's commanding officer and a senior officer in handling a welfare check and subsequent investigation is under review.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police eyewitnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, neither of the subject officers agreed to interviews with the SIU. One of the officers did provide a written statement. In the late evening of early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a motor vehicle accident at an intersection in the area. The collision involved a single vehicle that had been stolen moments earlier. Witnesses to the collision reported that the driver and sole occupant of the vehicle – the affected person – had fled the area. The affected person had run from the site of the collision to a nearby residential street and ultimately made his way to the rear of a property where they hid in a backyard shed. A police dog unit was deployed to the area and eventually tracked the affected person to the shed. One of the officers who had been searching for the affected person called out to them but there was no response. The officer eventually opened the door to the shed and told the affected person to show their hands. When the affected person, lying on the ground, failed to do so, the officer discharged their CEW. As the affected person cried out in pain, the officer dragged them out of the shed, delivering a punch to the torso in the process. Other officers arrived on scene and joined in the arrest process. Repeated directions were given to the affected person to show and release their hands. The officer struck the affected person again in the torso and another officer punched them in the head or upper body area multiple times. Following another CEW discharge by the officer into the affected person’s back, their hands were controlled and handcuffed. The affected person was seen at hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with injuries.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2025, police officers, including the subject officer, attended at a campsite in a provincial park. A park warden had contacted police for their assistance. A short period prior, the park warden, at the campsite in connection with a noise complaint, had witnessed one of the campers - the affected person - slap the park warden in the face. The subject officer and another officer took custody of the affected person and handcuffed them behind the back without incident. The affected person was searched and placed in the rear of the police cruiser for transportation to the detachment. At the detachment, the affected person was lodged in a cell and held there until their release from custody later that morning. The affected person returned to the park to meet with the park warden in violation of a no-contact condition of their release from custody. They subsequently attended hospital that same day and were diagnosed with two broken ribs.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In the morning of early 2026, members of the Emergency Response Team convened at a house situated in the area of Cornwall. Acting under the authority of a search warrant, the team entered the house to search for cocaine and fentanyl. The affected person was present at the time and alerted to the officers’ presence outside the home. They entered the main floor bathroom and attempted to conceal themselves behind a sink. They were quickly located by an officer and placed on the floor in a prone position. The subject officer, the team leader, had forced their way into the bathroom and located the affected person. Having positioned the affected person on the floor, the officer dropped their left knee intending to lower themselves by their side but inadvertently landed on the affected person’s back. The impact fractured the affected person’s left hand, which was underneath their chest at the time. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to the police station. They were seen at hospital later in the day and diagnosed with their injury.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the early morning of early 2026, an officer and the subject officer were dispatched to an area in Toronto, following a 911 call. The caller did not address the call-taker but utterances were heard in the background suggesting an ongoing and potentially violent disturbance involving a male and female. The officers entered the home despite objections from the woman who opened the door – the affected person. The affected person was placed under arrest by an officer after they observed a male – the affected person – with a cut to the forehead. The affected person refused to cooperate as the officer sought to question them about what had happened. Standing on a second floor landing, the affected person asked the officers to leave and then called 911 asking for additional officers. The officers told the affected person to disconnect the 911 call as they were the police. When they failed to do so, they warned that they would be arrested for public mischief. Shortly after, the officers took hold of the affected person to take them into custody. The affected person refused to surrender their arms to be handcuffed and was grounded. On the floor, they resisted the officers’ efforts to control their arms behind the back and was met with a punch to the face by the subject officer. Moments later, as the struggle continued, the subject officer delivered two punches to the affected person’s head in quick succession. It would take another five minutes, during which an officer kicked the affected person once or twice in the legs and exhorted them verbally to give up their arms, before the officers wrestled control of their arms behind the back and secured them in handcuffs. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the late afternoon of early 2026, officers were alerted to a recent theft of alcohol from a liquor store. The suspect had fled the store and entered a nearby retail location where a security guard stopped him. The suspect dropped a backpack containing stolen items and fled further into the store as the officers arrived. The suspect was chased, grounded, and handcuffed. An officer arrived shortly after the arrest to assist and was checking the backpack when they were approached by a person. This person picked up an item that had fallen from the backpack, claiming it belonged to them. The officer replied that it was not theirs, stood up, and removed the item from the person's hand. The person swiped the officer's hand away, and then swiped it away again as the officer reached out to grab them. The officer moved towards the person, grabbed them by the upper body, and used their leg to sweep the person's legs out from under them. The person fell onto their back and immediately favoured their right knee. They were handcuffed and taken into custody. The person was seen at hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fractured right knee.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, the subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. Police officers were dispatched to an area in Toronto, in the early morning, after a call was made to police following a concern about the affected person. The affected person was prohibited from communicating or being with another person as a condition of a release order. The subject officer arrived on scene with other officers. The affected person was located and asked to identify themselves. They refused to do so but entered a residence with the officers. The officers were able to ascertain the affected person's identity and moved to take them into custody. A struggle ensued and the affected person was forced to the floor by the officers, after which they were secured in handcuffs. The affected person was injured in the takedown and transported to hospital after their arrest and treated for their injury.

summary_redacted: In the evening of early 2026, police officers were on the lookout for a vehicle being operated by someone who was possibly impaired. Police received information that a suspected impaired driver had left an area. About ten minutes later, the subject officer radioed that they had located the vehicle and were pursuing it. The driver did not stop for the officer. Instead, they led the officer on a chase during which they disregarded stop signs and traffic lights, and travelled well over the speed limit, at times on the opposite side of the road. Having crossed a bridge, the driver continued at pace and lost control of the vehicle, slamming into a utility pole at an intersection. The driver was transported to hospital from the scene and diagnosed with injuries. The subject officer was a few car lengths away from the driver at the time of the collision. They had tracked the vehicle over a distance, at times well over the speed limit and, on at least two occasions, through red lights without first coming to a stop.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the early afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer was dispatched to a gas station following a call to police about a male kicking a door at the premises. The subject officer arrived on scene, parked their cruiser in the parking lot and located the male – the affected person. They detained the affected person and subsequently arrested them on the strength of several outstanding warrants. The subject officer handcuffed the affected person behind the back and conducted a pat-down search by the side of the cruiser, confiscating several items, after which they placed them in the rear of their vehicle. Another officer arrived on scene to take custody of the affected person and transport them to the station. They and the subject officer approached the rear of the subject officer's cruiser, opened the passenger door and observed that the affected person had brought their handcuffed hands to the front and their shoes were off. The officers removed the affected person and subjected them to a secondary search. On this occasion, they located vials and substances in the inner pockets of the affected person's coat. Following the search, en route to the station, the officer observed that something was in the affected person's mouth. They said that they had consumed fentanyl, at which point the officer pulled over and called for an ambulance. While waiting for the ambulance, the officer pleaded with the affected person to spit out what was in their mouth. Other officers arrived on scene and did the same. The affected person said that they would rather die than go to police cells. Paramedics attended and transported the affected person to hospital. They were unresponsive with persistent bradycardia on arrival. They were monitored overnight and released into custody the following day.

The jury returned a verdict in 2025, and issued recommendations to the police service regarding medical professional integration, biometric monitoring, documentation, staff training on withdrawal symptoms, and operational debriefs following deaths in custody.
tribunal_body: Office of the Chief Coroner — Ontario (Inquest: Christopher Campbell and Fabian Hart)

summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may be briefly summarized. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person was with her boyfriend, an off-duty officer, on a street in Toronto when the two quarreled and she left the area telling him that she was going to jump. She made her way to a bridge over a river and called the officer using a passerby’s cellphone. She told him she was going to commit suicide and that it would be his fault. The officer called police to report that the affected person was in mental health crisis and threatening to harm herself. He would subsequently learn from a third-party whose phone the affected person had used to call him that she was in the area of the bridge and a roadway. The subject officer drove in that direction, travelling across the bridge. With him was another officer. The subject officer approached the side of the bridge, brought his vehicle to a stop and exited with the other officer. They were just out of their cruiser when the affected person scaled the bridge guardrail and jumped down the other side. She landed on the frozen river below, a distance of about eight metres. The two officers made their way to the riverbank and walked onto the river to render aid to the affected person. Firefighters attended the scene and assisted in her removal. She was transported to hospital and diagnosed with fractures and injuries to her internal organs.

summary_redacted: A public interest investigation was conducted into the use of a conducted energy weapon by a member of the RCMP in Inuvik, Northwest Territories. The investigation followed a complaint respecting an incident that occurred on March 13, 2007, at the Arctic Tern Youth Facility. The officer attended the facility in response to a call for assistance in dealing with a female inmate. Upon arrival, the officer found the inmate lying face down with her hands cuffed behind her back, with several youth officers present. The incident led to an investigation under the RCMP Act Subsection 45.46(3).
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2008-3149 — Chair's Final Report After Commissioner's Notice – Report Following a Public Interest Investigation into a Chair-Initiated Complaint Respecting the Use of a Conducted Energy Weapon in Inuvik, Northwest Territories

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted: A civilian contractor working on a Canadian Forces Base was improperly detained, handcuffed, and placed in an interview room by Military Police members after a CPIC query returned an immigration warrant belonging to a different person. The Military Police Complaints Commission found that the detention lacked reasonable suspicion, that the manner of detention was disproportionate, and that the conduct constituted racial profiling. The CFPM's Office of Professional Standards had originally found the allegations unsubstantiated; following MPCC review, multiple allegations were found supported by the evidence. The MPCC issued twenty recommendations addressing individual conduct and systemic deficiencies in CPIC procedures, racial profiling training, evidence preservation, and supervisory oversight.
A person and a child died by drowning after a tractor sank into the ice on a lake in a rural area. The weight of the tractor exceeded the capacity of the ice at the accident site.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a city. The person who called 911 wanted the affected person removed from their residence. The officer arrived on scene with another officer shortly before 11:00 p.m., joined shortly by a third officer. The person who called 911 met them outside the building and provided them a key to the apartment. One of the officers opened the door, stepped inside and was splashed with cooking oil from a bottle by the affected person. The affected person retreated into the kitchen area and was physically engaged by the officers. They were taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back by the officer. The affected person was placed in the backseat of the officer's cruiser and transported to a police detention facility. En route, they slipped the handcuffs under their legs so that they were then positioned in front of their body. In statements made to police personnel at the station, they would explain that the pain in their left wrist was the result of this maneuver. The next day, the affected person was seen at a hospital and diagnosed with a fractured left wrist.

summary_redacted: A complaint was filed with the Military Police Complaints Commission alleging that Military Police at an airfield in Afghanistan failed to investigate possible detainee abuse. Three individuals detained by forces on or around early 2006 in a region were transferred to Military Police with documented injuries including contusions, abrasions, and lacerations. The complaint alleges Military Police failed to arrange medical examinations, failed to properly inventory detainees' personal effects, and transferred the injured detainees to local police without conducting a forensic medical inquiry, contrary to standard procedures and the detainee transfer arrangement between forces and Afghanistan.
tribunal_body: Military Police Complaints Commission — Afghanistan Public Interest Investigation

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officers chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2026, the subject officers, in the company of other officers, attended at an apartment in Oshawa. A neighbouring tenant had called 911 to report the sounds of a woman screaming coming from the apartment. Officers knocked on the door and asked the woman to open it. She tried but was unable to open the door. She could be heard moaning in distress and saying that she had been choked. An officer retrieved a ram from their cruiser and the subject officer used it to break open the door. The woman was by the doorway and pointed in the direction of a hallway. The affected person appeared in the hallway. He was at the apartment in violation of a bail order. An officer ordered the affected person to put his hands behind his back. The affected person refused to do so and instead walked towards the officer. The officer grabbed a hold of the affected person and attempted to wrestle control of his arms behind his back. The affected person refused to release his arms. There followed a struggle during which the affected person was forced to the hallway floor and subjected to multiple punches by the officers, and the use of a CEW, before he was handcuffed behind the back. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to hospital on two occasions before he was eventually diagnosed with a broken rib.

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted_fr: Une allégation d'agression sexuelle sur la personne d'un cadet mineur au camp de cadets en Colombie-Britannique au cours de l'été 2004 a fait l'objet d'une enquête par le Service national des enquêtes des Forces canadiennes, région de l'Ouest. La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a tenu une audience d'intérêt public et conclu que l'enquête n'avait pas été menée avec diligence ni compétence, que l'interrogatoire du jeune suspect avait été conduit de façon inadéquate, que des éléments disculpatoires avaient été omis du dossier de la Couronne, et que des défaillances systémiques graves existaient en matière de formation, de procédures opérationnelles et de supervision. Des mesures correctives sous forme de conseils et de formation d'appoint ont été ordonnées à l'endroit des deux enquêteurs impliqués.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire — CPPM-2005-024

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and three police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, neither subject officer agreed to an interview with the SIU. One of the subject officers did authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of late 2025, the affected person was arrested for drug trafficking following a traffic stop by police officers. A warrant authorizing a search of the affected person's residence for evidence of drug trafficking was in effect at the time. An officer searched the affected person at the scene and confiscated a quantity of drugs from their clothes. The affected person was transported to the police station and subjected to another search of their clothing, this time with negative results. They were lodged in a cell and began to vomit. Paramedics arrived at the station and the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with opioid withdrawal.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of October 2025, the subject officer was off-duty and attending an appointment at a business in Thunder Bay. While there, they observed a person applying orange spray paint to the front door of the business. They alerted the owner, who ran after the affected person, and the subject officer followed. As the affected person fled, they lost their footing and fell forward onto the sidewalk, striking their face. The owner and the subject officer reached them within moments where they had fallen. The owner attempted to secure the affected person's legs, during which the affected person kicked at them. The subject officer reached towards the affected person's upper body as they attempted to gain control of them, the affected person continued to kick. The subject officer positioned the affected person onto their right side, and as they applied physical control, including the use of their bodyweight, the affected person came to rest in a prone position. The subject officer maintained control over the affected person's upper back. The subject officer and the owner then brought the affected person's hands behind them and continued to restrain them until police arrived. Several bystanders had gathered, and one contacted police. A police officer responded, arrested the affected person for mischief, assisted them to their feet, and transported them to the police station. The following day, the affected person attended hospital and was diagnosed with bilateral nasal bone fractures of indeterminate age.

A person died from medical complications after a fall in a care facility. The coroner's investigation found that the risk of falls was not properly assessed and the sensitive mat was defective. The coroner made recommendations to improve clinical documentation and risk assessment.

A person died from complications related to Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, in the context of unintentional methotrexate poisoning, in Quebec. The person had several medical antecedents that greatly weakened them. The circumstances surrounding the death raise concerns about the surveillance of signs of drug toxicity in patients with comorbidities and treated with methotrexate.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a residence. A resident had called police to remove an intoxicated and unwelcome guest – the affected person – who was refusing to leave. Officers arrived on scene and took a belligerent affected person into custody to prevent a breach of the peace. They were placed in a cruiser and transported to the police detachment. The affected person remained confrontational at the detachment. At one point, after they had been stood facing a wall to be searched, the affected person turned and started to close their hand in the direction of one of the searching officers. The officer standing behind the affected person reacted by wrapping an arm around their neck, forcing them to the floor and delivering a punch in the direction of their upper body. The affected person, prone on the floor, did not readily release their arms at the officers’ direction and was met with several punches to the legs and torso by the officers. The officers wrestled control of their arms after the strikes and handcuffed them behind their back. The affected person was subsequently transported to hospital when they complained of facial pain. They were diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police eyewitnesses, and video footage that largely captured the events in question, gives rise to the following scenario.  As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of late 2025, acting on the authority of a Form 2 issued under the Mental Health Act, police officers attended an apartment in the area of a city.  The form, authorizing police to compel the affected person’s attendance at hospital for a psychiatric examination, had been obtained.  The person who obtained the form had become increasingly concerned for the affected person’s well-being. Arriving at about morning time in front of the apartment door, officers attempted to have the affected person exit the apartment.  They explained who they were and why they were there.  The affected person adamantly refused to leave their apartment.  Behind a barricaded front door, the affected person variously threatened police that they would jump from the balcony and harm them or themselves if they entered their residence.  Officers, including a member of the service’s crisis team, continued to negotiate with the affected person attempting to dissuade them from harming themselves or others.  They assured them that they would not face criminal charges, and that they would simply be escorting them to hospital.  The affected person remained unreceptive. With information that the affected person might jump from the balcony and was repeatedly threatening their life and those of the officers, the service deployed the emergency response unit to the scene.  The plan was to have officers rappel from the roof to the affected person’s balcony, preventing them from using it to jump from the building. Officers arrived and were harnessed and ready to descend from the rooftop.  As they were reaching the balcony railing, they were confronted by the affected person. With an aluminum baseball bat in hand, the affected person began to swing at the officers as they were still hanging from their rappel lines, striking one of them.  An officer managed to land on the balcony and immediately became engaged in a struggle with the affected person.  The two punched at each other, and the affected person fell to the ground. An officer deployed their CEW and another officer delivered a single punch to the face of the affected person as they lay on the ground.  Following the strike, the affected person’s hands were handcuffed. The affected person was taken to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with multiple facial fractures.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person made their way to a bridge. Their intention was to jump from the bridge. They called 911 to advise emergency responders of their intention and provided them their location. Two call-takers – one from a police service and another from the ambulance service – spoke to the affected person. They assured them they had done the right thing by calling them, asked them not to jump from the bridge, and told them help was on its way. The affected person stayed on the line for several minutes. At the very end, they told the police call-taker that they could hear and see responding police officers, and that they were going to jump. They proceeded to do just that. The subject officer was the first officer on scene. They stopped their cruiser in a parking lot by the bridge, exited and was making their way to the affected person’s position when the affected person jumped from the bridge onto some snow-covered rocks below. The officer provided care and comfort to the affected person while they waited for paramedics. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with fractures.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, a subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, a subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. Shortly before midnight, in early 2026, police were asked to assist in the search of a suspect who had fled the scene of an attempted break and enter in the Toronto area and entered into the Markham area with two accomplices in a vehicle. The vehicle had crashed on a street in the area, after which two of the suspects were arrested. The third took flight on foot and remained at large. Police deployed officers to the area of the collision and established a perimeter. Police dog teams were brought in to assist in the search, as was a police drone. Two subject officers were part of the police operations. They were paired with a dog handler, who conducted a track of the suspect across residential streets in the area and the rear yards of homes. At about 1:40 a.m., with the assistance of residents in the neighbourhood sharing their surveillance camera footage with the police, the subject officers and the dog handler located a male – the affected person – hiding underneath a tarp in the rear yard of a residence in the area. They alerted other officers, who arrived at the location to assist in taking the affected person into custody. The dog handler loudly announced that officers were present and directed the affected person to surrender. Initially, there was no response from the affected person. The officer warned that they would deploy their dog if the affected person did not surrender, at which point the affected person indicated they would come out. Another brief period passed before the subject officers moved in and physically engaged the affected person. They removed them from under the tarp and dragged them a distance away. The affected person was struck several times by the officers, including in the face, before they were handcuffed behind the back. Following their arrest, the affected person was seen at hospital and diagnosed with several facial fractures and a broken finger.

summary_redacted: In the afternoon of late 2025, the affected person attended a Canada Post outlet in a store in a town. They were there to pick up a key to a home. Staff at the outlet told them they could not be helped because they did not have the proper documentation. The affected person became belligerent and slammed their phone on the counter. Police were called to the scene. An officer was the first to arrive, joined shortly by another officer. This was the officers' second time at the outlet dealing with the affected person. Earlier that afternoon, they had attended to deal with the affected person, who had been refused service because of their behaviour and deficient paperwork. The affected person had left the store on that occasion. On the present occasion, an officer told the affected person to leave the store or they would be arrested, and then grabbed them to forcibly remove them from the store when they refused to exit of their own accord. The affected person physically resisted the officer's efforts to push them out and the two tussled briefly. Another officer grabbed the affected person and threw them to the floor, after which they were handcuffed behind the back. The affected person suffered an injury in the takedown. They were taken from the scene to hospital by paramedics.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police eyewitness, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, the subject officer and another officer were dispatched to a convenience store in the area of a major intersection. A store employee had contacted police to report a male and female shoplifter. The officers arrived on scene and spoke with store staff, confirming that two suspects had left the store with alcohol and food without paying for the items. The employee showed a video clip they had taken with their cellphone, depicting the male and female. The officers located the suspects – the other person and the affected person – at a nearby bus shelter. One person was taken into custody by one of the officers without incident. The affected person, however, resisted when the subject officer attempted to secure them in handcuffs. The officer was able to affix a cuff to the right hand but had difficulty attaching the other cuff to the affected person’s left hand. The affected person objected to their arrest, denied that they had done anything wrong, and struggled against the subject officer’s efforts to bring their arms behind the back. The tussle eventually made its way outside the bus shelter where the other officer intervened. The officers brought the affected person to the ground, after which the other officer secured both their hands in handcuffs. Following their arrest, the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for a fractured right wrist.

finding: Enquête fermée par la direction du BEI

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police eyewitness, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officers did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, the subject officer and the witness officer were dispatched to a convenience store in the area of a major road and avenue. A store employee had contacted police to report a male and female shoplifter. The officers arrived on scene and spoke with store staff, confirming that two suspects had left the store with alcohol and food without paying for the items. The employee showed a video clip they had taken with their cellphone, depicting the male and female. The officers located the suspects – the affected person and another individual – at a nearby bus shelter. One suspect was taken into custody by the witness officer without incident. The affected person, however, resisted when the subject officer attempted to secure them in handcuffs. The officer was able to affix a cuff to the right hand but had difficulty attaching the other cuff to the affected person’s left hand. The affected person objected to their arrest, denied that they had done anything wrong, and struggled against the subject officer’s efforts to bring their arms behind the back. The tussle eventually made its way outside the bus shelter where the witness officer intervened. The officers brought the affected person to the ground, after which the witness officer secured both their hands in handcuffs. Following their arrest, the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for a fractured right wrist.

summary_redacted: A person died from pulmonary overload secondary to atherosclerotic heart disease in their apartment at a seniors' residence in Gatineau. They had been hospitalized the day before for chest pain and respiratory problems but were sent home despite insufficient oxygen levels.

The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a residence. A resident had called police to remove an intoxicated and unwelcome guest – the complainant – who was refusing to leave. Officers arrived on scene and took a belligerent complainant into custody to prevent a breach of the peace. They were placed in a cruiser and transported to the police detachment. The complainant remained confrontational at the detachment. At one point, after they had been stood facing a wall to be searched, the complainant turned and started to close their hand in the direction of one of the searching officers. The officer standing behind the complainant reacted by wrapping an arm around their neck, forcing them to the floor and delivering a punch in the direction of their upper body. The complainant, prone on the floor, did not readily release their arms at the officers’ direction and was met with several punches to the legs and torso by the officers. The officers wrestled control of their arms after the strikes and handcuffed them behind the back. The complainant was subsequently transported to hospital when they complained of facial pain. They were diagnosed with a broken nose.

summary_redacted: A person died from a polytraumatism caused by an involuntary fall from a burning building in Gatineau. The fire, of accidental origin, was caused by an electrical short circuit linked to a poorly installed extension cord in their apartment.

The SIU investigated an incident involving Toronto Police Service (TPS) officers who attended at an address in the area of Jane Street and Wilson Avenue in Toronto in the evening of late December 2025. A civilian witness had called police to report being assaulted by the complainant, and had fled the apartment to a nearby location before officers arrived. Three witness officers arrived at the door of the apartment at approximately 11:40 p.m. and knocked and called out, receiving no response. While officers remained at the door awaiting a master key from the landlord, video footage from a camera on an adjacent building captured the complainant falling from height and impacting the ground at approximately 11:42 p.m. Officers entered the apartment after the key arrived shortly after midnight, and found the complainant was not inside. An officer then went onto the apartment balcony and observed the complainant on the ground below. First responders attended and the complainant was found to be deceased. The complainant had been intoxicated by drugs and alcohol in the period leading to their death. A pathologist attributed the cause of death to blunt impact injuries sustained from a descent from height. The SIU's evidence included a civilian witness interview and video footage that largely captured the incident.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police and non-police witnesses (including the affected person), and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. Two subject officers travelled to a residence in the area to arrest the affected person for criminal harassment. They had earlier spoken to a person, who reported that the affected person had stalked them since high school. The person was concerned for their safety, and the safety of their family as the affected person continued to attend their residence to shovel snow despite being told to stop. The affected person answered the officers’ knock on the door and was told they were to be arrested. They told the officers to get a warrant and closed the door, making their way to the basement. They called a family member, made remarks suggesting self-harm, and locked themselves in the basement bathroom where they proceeded to cut themselves. The officers were allowed into the residence by a witness, who expressed concern for the affected person’s safety. The officers went to the basement and started to speak to the affected person through the bathroom door. They told them they wanted to ensure their safety. The affected person said they were fine and taking a shower. The officers grew increasingly worried with the affected person’s wellbeing as their voice started to fade and attempted, unsuccessfully, to force open the door. Within minutes, additional officers arrived with a breach tool. The door was opened and officers entered. The affected person was inside the bathtub with knife wounds. Paramedics were called to the scene and transported them to hospital.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police eyewitnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, the affected person was at a nightclub in the Entertainment District. They were with friends to celebrate a birthday. They became involved in a disturbance with another group of patrons, and were escorted out of the premises by security staff. Unhappy with the manner in which security staff had treated them, one of the friends complained to officers who were outside the club policing the area. The subject officer was among the officers outside the nightclub. They and the friend had a brief verbal exchange before the affected person intervened and began to argue with the officer. The two were face to face and becoming more belligerent with each other. At one point, the subject officer told them to get out of the area or they would be arrested for public intoxication. Shortly after, the friend, standing beside the subject officer and the affected person, encroached on the officer's personal space in an aggressive fashion. The officer grabbed hold of the friend and was subsequently confronted by the affected person, who stepped in to try to separate the two. The subject officer turned their attention to the affected person and the two subsequently punched at each other before the officer grabbed hold of their head as other officers took hold of their arms. The officers attempted to take the affected person to the ground. The affected person struggled against their efforts and was met with several open-hand palm strikes to the abdomen and a couple of knees to the buttocks by another officer. Following those strikes, they were forced prone on the ground and handcuffed behind the back. The affected person was seen at hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a nasal fracture and a concussion.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

summary_redacted: A person died from injuries sustained in a collision with a vehicle while cycling in a region. The collision occurred after the bicycle hit a pothole and lost control. The coroner's report raises concerns about the quality of road paving and the safety of cyclists on roads.

The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and the subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. In early 2026, the affected person was arrested by the subject officer and another officer inside a residence. The officers had gone to the location following a call to police about smoke coming from the residence. The affected person was unlawfully inside the abandoned home. They had stepped in seeking shelter from the cold outside, and had started a fire in a device to warm themselves. The affected person was searched by the subject officer at the scene and again at a police detachment. Some drug paraphernalia was confiscated. Asked whether they had consumed illicit substances in the last 24 hours, the affected person said they had not. The affected person was lodged in a cell. Later, upon returning to their cell from a phone room where they had contacted a lawyer, the affected person retrieved fentanyl from vials secreted in their groin area and consumed a quantity of the drug. They began to suffer the effects of the drug and became unresponsive on the cell floor. The subject officer entered the cell within minutes of the affected person's medical event and attempted to rouse them with a sternum rub. They placed the affected person in the recovery position, administered a couple of doses of nasal naloxone and contacted EMS. Paramedics arrived on scene and took charge of the affected person's care. They were transported to hospital and treated for acute fentanyl overdose.

Deux plaintes font suite à un incident où un officier de la police militaire aurait été intercepté alors qu'il tentait de conduire avec les facultés affaiblies en présence de ses enfants ; il est allégué que la direction du détachement a fait pression sur les enquêteurs pour supprimer les accusations et dissimulé l'affaire, et que des modifications non autorisées ont été apportées aux notes d'enquête. finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected gives rise to the following scenario. In the evening of late 2025, police officers were dispatched to a location. The police had received a call about a disturbance involving the affected person and their partner. The subject officer arrived on scene and was joined by other officers. A crisis worker was also in attendance with the police. The subject officer was met by the affected person and allowed inside. The two made their way to the rear yard to talk. The other officers remained in the house to speak with the partner. Over the course of the next 45 minutes, the subject officer attempted to have the affected person explain what had happened. An intoxicated affected person engaged with the officer in fits and starts, unable to provide a coherent account of the events and occasionally adopting a belligerent posture. The officer told the affected person that they would not be allowed to stay at the location that night and would need to explore alternative accommodations. The affected person became increasingly frustrated, unable to secure themselves a place for the night. Near the end of their discourse, the affected person walked towards the rear door of the location. The subject officer grabbed a hold of them, told them they were under arrest for public intoxication, and attempted to pull them away from the location. The affected person resisted the officer by pulling in the opposite direction. There followed an altercation. The subject officer threw the affected person to the ground and positioned themselves overtop their supine torso. Another officer was by the affected person's upper body. The affected person thrashed their legs about. The subject officer delivered a series of elbow strikes to the affected person's face, after which their arms were controlled in front of their body and handcuffed. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fractured shoulder.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, the affected person was at a public square to protest against a demonstration by a movement. The demonstrators had made their way to the public square from another location, where they had initially gathered. The affected person, with a group of other people, had also been at the other location attempting to disrupt the demonstration. Police officers had been assigned to police the demonstration and counter-protest. They were to preserve the peace and attempt to keep the rival factions separated. At the public square, officers used their bicycles to erect a perimeter between the demonstrators, at the centre of the square, and the counter-protesters, on its periphery. The affected person was in the area of the northeast corner of the square when they and others were asked to leave the square. The affected person began to slowly walk away but stopped to object to the treatment of a person, who was being physically escorted by police in a certain direction. The subject officer ordered them to leave or they would be arrested for obstructing police. The officer then pushed the affected person by the arms towards a roadway, the roadway that bordered the square. The affected person told the subject officer that they did not have to touch them and turned to face the officer. The subject officer reacted by turning them around and pushing them in the back towards the roadway. The affected person stumbled forward and fell onto the sidewalk, fracturing their hip in the process. The affected person was transported by paramedics to hospital and treated for their injury.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the afternoon, in a city, the subject officer observed the affected person in an area. Aware that the affected person was subject to a warrant for their arrest, the officer contacted uniformed personnel to respond to the area to take the affected person into custody. A witness officer arrived in the area in a fully marked cruiser. The officer pulled up to the affected person as they walked and watched as the affected person reversed directions and ran. The subject officer had stopped their vehicle a short distance away and exited, running across the street and tackling the affected person to the ground. The affected person attempted to right themselves and was held down by the subject officer and the witness officer, who arrived on scene within seconds. There ensued a struggle during which the subject officer delivered several punches to the head and torso of the affected person, and a strike to their side. With the assistance of additional officers arriving on scene, the affected person was handcuffed behind the back. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the early afternoon of a day in early 2026, the subject officer was dispatched to a gas station following a call to police about a male kicking a door at the premises. The subject officer arrived on scene, parked their cruiser in the parking lot, and located the affected person. They detained the affected person and subsequently arrested them on the strength of several outstanding warrants. The subject officer handcuffed the affected person behind the back and conducted a pat-down search by the side of the cruiser, confiscating several items, after which they placed them in the rear of their vehicle. Another officer arrived on scene and was to take custody of the affected person and transport them to the station. They and the subject officer approached the rear of the subject officer's cruiser, opened the passenger door, and observed that the affected person had brought their handcuffed hands to the front and their shoes were off. The officers removed the affected person and subjected them to a secondary search. On this occasion, they located vials and substances in the inner pockets of the affected person's coat. Following the search, en route to the station, the officer observed that something was in the affected person's mouth. They said that they had consumed a substance, at which point the officer pulled over and called for an ambulance. While waiting for the ambulance, the officer pleaded with the affected person to spit out what was in their mouth. Other officers arrived on scene and did the same. The affected person said that they would rather die than go to police cells. Paramedics attended and transported the affected person to hospital. They were unresponsive with persistent bradycardia on arrival. They were monitored overnight and released into the custody of the authorities the following day.

finding: Enquête fermée par la direction du BEI
