summary_redacted: In a rural British Columbia city, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police were involved in a shooting death. A mental health warrant had been issued to apprehend and return the affected person to the hospital for psychiatric treatment. The RCMP members breached the door to the affected person's apartment and entered with their guns drawn. The affected person, who was not acting rationally, engaged the officers with two knives in their hands. A conducted energy weapon was used. The incident led to a Chair-Initiated Public Complaint under the Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act. The Commission reviewed the facts surrounding the events, including the RCMP's response to the mental health warrant, risk assessment, and plan to apprehend the affected person. The Commission also examined the initial investigative response and the timeliness of the investigation. The incident resulted in the death of the affected person.
tribunal_body: CRCC file 2007-10404 — Interim Report on Chair-Initiated Public Complaint regarding the Shooting Death of Christopher Klim, Vernon, B.C.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and a subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, a subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, two subject officers were on-duty together assigned to a neighbourhood resource team, a proactive policing initiative by the service. The officers attended an apartment complex in the area to perform welfare checks, enforce conditions of release and execute outstanding arrest warrants. The complainant was identified as a person associated with the building who was subject to an arrest warrant on theft charges. The officers were just outside the elevator when it opened and the complainant stepped out. Asked to identify themselves, the complainant provided a false name and attempted to walk by the officers. The officers took hold of the complainant and a struggle ensued. The complainant was forcibly taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back. The complainant was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fractured left knee.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question, clear on the evidence collected by the SIU, may briefly be summarized. In the morning of late 2025, police officers, including two subject officers, were dispatched to an area in Hamilton, following a call to police about a violent incident. The affected person, in violation of a no-contact order, had visited the affected person's partner at a location and struck them in the face. The affected person and their partner had left by the time of the officers’ arrival. The officers interviewed witnesses and determined there were grounds to arrest the affected person for assault. The affected person and their partner had traveled to the affected person’s residence and were there when the officers arrived on scene shortly after 7:00 a.m. The witness officer and another officer were also present. Another officer heard a female screaming from inside the residence and alerted the other officers. Led by one of the subject officers, the officers knocked on the door and directed the affected person to come out. The affected person refused to allow them entry into the residence, asserting it was their home and they needed a warrant. The officers explained that they had exigent circumstances and demanded that they open the door or they would force their way inside. When the affected person continued to refuse, one of the subject officers used a baton to smash the door’s glass window. Shortly after the window was broken, the affected person opened the door. The officers quickly grabbed the affected person and pulled them forward. The affected person stepped from the residence floor to ground level, landing awkwardly on their foot and fracturing it in the process. They were placed in a prone position on the ground and handcuffed without incident. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and treated for their foot fractures.

summary_redacted_fr: Le Commissaire à la déontologie policière a pris connaissance d'une situation médiatique concernant une intervention effectuée par plusieurs policiers du Service de police de la Ville de Montréal auprès d'un individu. Une enquête déontologique a été décrétée afin d'examiner si la conduite des policiers est susceptible de constituer un acte dérogatoire au Code de déontologie des policiers du Québec. L'enquête est en cours.
tribunal_body: Commissaire à la déontologie policière — dossier COM 26-6001

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was his legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. He did authorize the release of his notes. In early 2026, two officers were dispatched to a location in Toronto following a 911 call. The caller did not address the call-taker but utterances were heard in the background suggesting an ongoing and potentially violent disturbance involving a male and female. The officers entered the home despite objections from the woman who opened the door – the affected person. The affected person was placed under arrest by one of the officers after he observed a male – the affected person – with a cut to the forehead. The affected person refused to cooperate as the officer sought to question him about what had happened. Standing on a second floor landing, the affected person asked the officers to leave and then called 911 asking for additional officers. The officers told the affected person to disconnect the 911 call as they were the police. When he failed to do so, they warned that he would be arrested for public mischief. Shortly after, the officers took hold of the affected person to take him into custody. The affected person refused to surrender his arms to be handcuffed and was grounded. On the floor, he resisted the officers’ efforts to control his arms behind the back and was met with a punch to the face by one of the officers. Moments later, as the struggle continued, the officer delivered two punches to the affected person’s head in quick succession. It would take another five minutes, during which the other officer kicked the affected person once or twice in the legs and exhorted him verbally to give up his arms, before the officers wrestled control of his arms behind the back and secured them in handcuffs. The affected person was transported to hospital after his arrest and diagnosed with a broken nose.

A pedestrian died after being struck by a vehicle while crossing the street outside an intersection in a city. The coroner's office recommends that the relevant authority intensify education efforts to promote safe pedestrian behaviors, such as making eye contact with drivers before crossing and using intersections.

A person died in a collision on a route in a region after their vehicle crossed into the opposite lane, likely due to drowsiness or distraction, and struck another vehicle. The coroner's office made recommendations to improve road safety.

A person died from probable cardiac arrhythmia caused by severe hyperkalemia at their home in a rural area. Despite concerning clinical signs observed in the preceding days, the treating physician was not informed in time of critical laboratory results due to a change in priority and a postal strike.

A person died after being struck by a metro in a city. An investigation found that the person had a history of mental health issues and substance abuse, and had been in contact with health services and community resources. Recommendations were made to improve services for young people and prevent suicide in the metro.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, neither subject officer agreed to interviews with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. Shortly before midnight, in late 2025, a team of police officers, including two subject officers, were dispatched to a house in the area of a city, the residence of the affected person. They arrived intending to arrest the affected person under the Mental Health Act following a number of calls to police in which they were reported to have attacked motorists, brandishing a knife in one instance. The affected person refused to surrender to police. Over the course of the next couple of hours, they would exit and re-enter their home through the front door, challenging the officers gathered by the road outside their house. They told them they would have to engage them physically to take them into custody. At about 2:20 a.m., as the affected person was two to three metres away from their front door gesticulating at the officers on the roadway, the two subject officers, in the company of two witness officers, approached them. One of the subject officers attempted to distract the affected person, who reacted by turning to run towards the front porch and door. The subject officers and witness officers discharged their CEWs and also attempted to distract the affected person as they made it onto the front porch but no further. One of the subject officers cut them off before they could re-enter the house, using their arm to tackle them against the wall adjacent the front door. Following additional CEW discharges, the affected person’s arms were handcuffed behind their back. The affected person was seen at hospital after their arrest and treated for a deep laceration to the scalp.

summary_redacted: A person died after a fall in their hospital room in a city, raising questions about the quality of care provided during their hospitalization.

A person died from complications related to a hip fracture sustained after a fall in an intermediate resource. The circumstances surrounding the death raise concerns about the preservation of motor skills in elderly people in isolation and the assessment of the risk of falls.

A person died from a secondary slipping syndrome due to dehydration and a superinfected pressure sore in a region. The affected person's general condition had deteriorated in the month preceding their hospitalization, with significant weight loss, decreased food intake, and the appearance of a severe sacral sore. The presence of this sore had not been documented before their admission to the emergency room, raising questions about the follow-up and care received in the residence.

A person died of sepsis in a private seniors' residence in a city while waiting for a place in a long-term care facility. The circumstances surrounding the death raise concerns about the coordination of care, the absence of nursing evaluation, and the management of interterritorial relocations.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person's police custodians, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officials did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the afternoon of late 2025, the affected person was arrested in connection with a stolen automobile. They were searched at the scene and transported to the police headquarters where they were lodged in a cell at about 5:45 p.m. They told police that they had consumed heroin a couple of hours before their arrest and explained they would become sick in their cell once the effects of the drug wore off. The affected person was monitored by special constables while in cells. They appeared to sleep for most of their time in custody. At about 7:40 a.m., the affected person had just woken when they started to vomit. They were removed from the cell and transported to hospital. The affected person was taken to hospital and treated for opioid withdrawal.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a city. The person who called 911 reported a disturbance involving the affected person. They wanted the affected person removed from their residence. The subject officer arrived on scene with another officer shortly before a certain time, joined shortly by a third officer. The person who called 911 met them outside the building and provided them a key to the apartment. One of the officers opened the door, stepped inside and was splashed with cooking oil from a bottle by the affected person. The affected person retreated into the kitchen area and was physically engaged by the officers. They were taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back by the subject officer. The affected person was placed in the backseat of the subject officer's cruiser and transported to a police detention facility. En route, they slipped the handcuffs under their legs so that they were then positioned in front of their body. In statements made to police personnel at the station, they would explain that the pain in their left wrist was the result of this maneuver. The next day, the affected person was seen at a hospital and diagnosed with a fractured left wrist.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, staff at a housing support centre in a regional town contacted police to have a woman – the affected person – removed from the facility. The affected person was causing a disturbance and had behaved rudely to staff. They were subject to an order prohibiting their attendance at the centre. The officer arrived on scene and approached the affected person. They were seated on a sofa in the reception area. The officer stood a distance from them and explained that they needed to leave. The affected person refused to leave. After a period of further conversation, the officer approached the affected person and took hold of their arm. The affected person moved down the sofa away from the officer. They flailed their legs at the officer and fought against their efforts to take them into custody. The struggle moved to the floor where the officer attempted to wrestle control of their arms behind their back. The affected person resisted strenuously for a period of time, even as the officer tried to keep them pinned to the floor with their knees and hands. It was only with the arrival of two additional officers that the affected person was handcuffed with their hands behind their back. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

summary_redacted: In the evening of early 2026, officers from the Ottawa Police Service responded to a reported domestic disturbance in the area of a major road and drive in Ottawa. They altered course to a nearby restaurant following a related call about a male on the roof of the building. The subject officer was the first to arrive on scene, followed by two witness officers. The male on the roof was the affected person, who was of unsound mind. They had earlier called police from their residence, reporting a break-in attempt. They exited through a window, made their way to the restaurant, and ascended onto the roof. The subject officer and a witness officer attempted to persuade them to come down safely. The affected person threatened to jump if not provided with a cigarette and hung from a light bar attached to the exterior wall. As negotiations were ongoing, a fire truck arrived on scene. The affected person lost their grip on the light bar and fell to the ground, landing feet-first. They were transported to hospital and diagnosed with multiple fractures of both feet.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2026, the affected person was driving in Brampton when a police cruiser started to follow their vehicle, attempting to pull them over. The affected person did not stop for the cruiser, turned into side streets, and ultimately lost control of their vehicle due to icy road conditions, striking parked cars. The affected person exited their car and fled the scene on foot. An officer was on patrol when their ALPR registered a hit on the affected person's vehicle, indicating that the registered owner was a suspended driver. The officer briefly chased the affected person on foot and broadcast that they were subject to arrest for failing to remain at the scene of an accident. The affected person made their way home to the area, where the subject officer was waiting and grabbed a hold of them, throwing them to the ground, just as they were attempting to enter a house. The officer kept the affected person pinned to the ground with their body weight for a period before being joined by another officer. The affected person struggled against the officers' efforts to control their arms and bit one of the officers. The subject officer kneed the affected person in the left ribs and pulled them onto their left side. The parties continued to wrestle for control of the affected person's arms, and the subject officer delivered another knee strike to the torso. With the arrival and intervention of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed and taken into custody. The affected person was charged with multiple offences and released at the scene, subsequently attending hospital and being diagnosed with a fractured left rib.

The Chair of the Commission for Public Complaints Against the RCMP filed a complaint regarding the shooting death of a person. On a date in 2004, the person broke into a pharmacy in a mall in Vanderhoof, British Columbia. A constable, a member of the Vanderhoof RCMP, responded to the break-in alarm. Another constable, also with the Vanderhoof RCMP, responded to the alarm in a separate police vehicle. The first constable observed the person running from the mall and pursued them in their police vehicle. The constable exited their police vehicle near a curling rink and continued to pursue the person on foot. The constable followed the person onto a soccer field while yelling commands for them to stop. The person eventually stopped, turned around and came back towards the constable. The incident was reviewed under subsection 45.46(3) of the RCMP Act.
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2006-0385 — Chair's Final Report – Shooting Death of Mr. Kevin St. Arnaud

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and a subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, one of the subject officers did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, two subject officers were on-duty together assigned to a neighbourhood resource team, a proactive policing initiative by the service. The officers attended an apartment complex in the area to perform welfare checks, enforce conditions of release and execute outstanding arrest warrants. The affected person was identified as a person associated with the building who was subject to an arrest warrant on theft charges. The officers were just outside the elevator when it opened and the affected person stepped out. Asked to identify themselves, the affected person provided a false name and attempted to walk by the officers. The officers took hold of them and a struggle ensued. The affected person was forcibly taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fractured left knee.

summary_redacted: The Canadian Forces National Investigation Service Western Region (CFNIS WR) investigated two sexual assault allegations made in late 2021 against a Canadian Forces officer posted to a base in Saskatchewan. Following a short investigation, the officer was arrested and charged with two counts of sexual assault; they died by suicide in early 2022. The Military Police Complaints Commission conducted a Public Interest Investigation and found the CFNIS WR investigation was inadequate, marked by undue haste, confirmation bias, and tunnel vision. The CFPM declined to implement any of the recommendations issued by the MPCC Chairperson in a final report issued in 2026.
tribunal_body: Military Police Complaints Commission — MPCC 2022-017 and MPCC 2022-041

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the evening, officers responded to a reported domestic disturbance in the area of a city, altering course to a nearby location following a related call about a male on the roof of a building. The subject officer was the first to arrive on scene, followed by two witness officers. The male on the roof was the affected person, who had earlier called police from their residence, reporting a break-in attempt. The affected person exited their residence through a window and made their way to the location, ascending onto the roof and perching themselves at its highest point. They would eventually remove their pants, wearing only underwear. The subject officer and a witness officer attempted to persuade the affected person to come down safely. The affected person threatened to jump if not provided a cigarette and hung from a light bar attached to the top of the exterior wall. As negotiations were ongoing, a fire truck appeared on scene. About seven minutes after the arrival of the police, the affected person again hung on the exterior wall, holding the light bar, lost their grip, and fell to the ground, landing feet-first. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with multiple fractures of both feet.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In late 2025, the subject officer and the witness officer were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a city in Ontario, following calls to police from the resident, the affected person. The affected person threatened to kill herself if police did not attend her residence and indicated that she had cut her wrists. The officers arrived at the location, where they were joined by paramedics. The first responders made their way to the apartment door. Finding it locked, the subject officer kicked it open. The affected person was in her bedroom at the time. She was highly agitated and belligerent with the officers. A cut was visible on her wrist. The officers handcuffed the affected person behind the back and were escorting her down a hallway in the apartment when the witness officer briefly pinned her against a wall as the affected person’s hostility escalated. The affected person turned and spat on the witness officer, and was taken to the floor by the officers in a prone position. The affected person screamed at the officers to kill her and banged her face on the floor about a half-dozen times, fracturing her nose in the process. The subject officer took hold of the affected person’s head to prevent her hurting herself. The affected person was seen at hospital after her arrest and ultimately diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: Just after midnight in late December 2025, Toronto Police Service (TPS) officers were called to the scene of a reported hit and run in a residential area of the city. A woman on the roadway had been struck by a vehicle — a Ford Edge — that had left and then returned to the scene. Firefighters were the first to arrive, followed shortly by the subject officer and their partner, a witness officer. A crowd of persons had gathered around the injured woman, including family members. The officers ordered the crowd back so the firefighters could render care. Within seconds of the officers' arrival, a family member of the injured woman approached the Ford Edge, parked nearby at the curb. They tried to open the door of the vehicle and were pushed away by the witness officer. The subject officer arrived at the driver's door of the Ford Edge, opened it, and took hold of the driver — the complainant. The subject officer detected the smell of alcohol emanating from the complainant. The officer repeatedly told the complainant to exit the vehicle and then forcibly removed them from their seat when they did not comply. The subject officer and witness officer escorted the complainant to the rear of the Ford Edge, where the subject officer grounded the complainant front first. The complainant was subsequently handcuffed behind the back and placed in the backseat of a police cruiser. The complainant was transported to hospital following their arrest and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In the morning of late 2025, the police received a call from hospital security reporting that a patient who had been discharged and escorted out of the hospital – the affected person – was in the parking lot, refusing to leave. The subject officer and another officer of the Emergency Response Team were dispatched. Upon arrival, the officers spoke with security and confirmed the information provided in the call. Security advised that the affected person had been discharged and provided with medication but refused to leave the hospital. They had to physically escort them out of the building; however, they still would not leave the property and wanted to return to the hospital. They were trespassed. Hospital staff did not want them back inside and wanted them removed. The briefing did not include information indicating that the affected person had a particular medical condition. The subject officer approached the affected person and informed them that they were required to leave the premises. The affected person stated that they wanted to return to the hospital because they were in pain. They were advised that they could seek care at another hospital, that they had been trespassed from this one, and that they must either leave or be arrested and removed from the premises. During the conversation, the affected person moved away from the vehicle where they had been standing and proceeded in the direction of the hospital doors. As they walked past, the subject officer asked where they were going and took hold of their arm. As the subject officer began to turn them around, an audible cracking sound was heard and the arm appeared to rotate in an abnormal manner consistent with having snapped. The affected person was permitted to re-enter the hospital so that their injury could be addressed. They were diagnosed with, and subsequently treated for, a comminuted and displaced fracture of the left distal humerus.

The agency's final report regarding a motor vehicle incident in a city in British Columbia, where a person died. On a date in 2012, members of the traffic section were conducting a targeted traffic enforcement operation in response to complaints from the public of speeding and aggressive driving in the area. The members involved were also using an Automated License Plate Recognition system to monitor passing traffic. Members attempted to stop a vehicle identified by the system as being associated with a prohibited driver. The vehicle failed to stop and fled. Two unmarked vehicles attempted to catch up to the fleeing vehicle but discontinued their attempts shortly thereafter. Within moments, the fleeing vehicle failed to stop at a stop sign at an intersection and collided with a semi-trailer truck. This collision caused the trailer to collide with a third vehicle driven by the affected person. The person sustained fatal injuries as a result of the collision and died at the scene.
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2013-0073 — Commission's Final Report after Commissioner's Response Regarding the Death of Mr. Victor Duarte in a Motor Vehicle Incident in Langley, British Columbia

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted:
The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer (SO) did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes.

In the evening in early November 2025, two witness officers (WO #1 and WO #2) were dispatched to a residence in the area of Ellesmere Road and Meadowvale Road, Toronto, in connection with a reported disturbance. The complainant had been quarrelling with another person and the argument had turned physical. The complainant was belligerent with the officers and refused to be assessed by attending paramedics.

At one point, while outside in front of the ambulance, the complainant swatted WO #1's hands. The officer reacted by pushing the complainant backward towards the ambulance and through the open side door. The two tussled briefly before WO #1 delivered a series of punches to the complainant's head area. Shortly after, the complainant was handcuffed and subsequently brought to 43 Division and lodged in a cell.

Early the following day, a special constable (SEW #1) attended the cell to retrieve the complainant to speak with duty counsel. The complainant refused to exit the cell and then prevented the special constable from closing the cell door by continually positioning a leg to block its closure. Additional officers attended and, following a struggle, removed the complainant from the cell in order to place them in another cell. The second cell had a "D" ring that could be used to further restrain the complainant while in the cell. The complainant refused to cooperate with the relocation and was dragged from the first cell to the second cell and forced inside.

Just inside the second cell, the complainant grabbed hold of the SO's left leg. The officer reacted by punching the complainant six times to the head, after which the complainant let go of the leg. Additional officers entered the cell and assisted in handcuffing the complainant to the "D" ring.

The complainant was transported to hospital later that same day. Their left eye was swollen and their nose had started bleeding. They were diagnosed with a fracture of the left orbital bone.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. Shortly before noon, in early 2026, police officers responded to a call in Sudbury. A 911 call had been received suggesting a potentially violent disturbance inside a vehicle. The caller did not communicate directly with the call-taker but the police were able to use her cell phone to approximate a location from where she was calling. An officer found the vehicle in the area. They spoke to two individuals and determined that there were grounds to believe that one of them had been assaulted by the affected person. Fearing their imminent arrest, the affected person, inside the rear of the vehicle, exited and fled the scene. They ran a short distance and entered into the rear, enclosed deck of a house in the area, where they sought to conceal themselves in a crawl space under a staircase. A police dog handler – the subject officer – and their dog arrived at the site of the vehicle from which the affected person had fled. Joined by other officers, the subject officer and their dog initiated a track of the affected person. They were not hard to find. The officers followed a set of footprints in the snow to the house in the area, spoke to one of the homeowners briefly, and entered the rear deck. The affected person was quickly located inside a crawl space. The subject officer released their police dog. The dog approached the affected person and bit their right hand. With the dog still latched onto their hand, the affected person was removed from the crawl space and positioned on the floor in a prone position. They were handcuffed behind the back and the dog separated from their right hand. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fracture of the small finger on the right hand.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and additional police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a location in response to a report of a person attempting to break into a residence. Two officers were the first on scene, arriving at about 3:10 a.m. They were joined shortly by a police dog handler, the subject officer, and their dog. Additional officers arrived on scene and set up a perimeter around the house as the subject officer deployed their dog to locate the suspect. It was not long before the dog led the subject officer to the rear yard and a crawl space underneath a deck. The affected person was hiding in the crawl space, which was cluttered with stored items. They failed to come out and give any indication of their presence despite repeated direction from the subject officer for them to do so. With the dog still attached to the lead, the subject officer released the dog into the crawl space. The dog’s first foray into the space was unsuccessful. The subject officer released the dog into the crawl space again. On this occasion, the dog located the affected person under some items and bit into their left arm. The affected person gave no reaction. The subject officer approached their dog and the affected person and released the dog’s hold, after which the affected person was taken into custody. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture and dog bites to the left arm.

summary_redacted: A person died from a severe head trauma caused by a fall from their motorized three-wheeler in Laval. They had a balance disorder and were not wearing a protective helmet at the time of the accident.

The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and the subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. In early 2026, the affected person was arrested by the subject officer and another officer inside a residence. The officers had gone to the location following a call to police about smoke coming from the residence. The affected person was unlawfully inside the abandoned home. They had stepped in seeking shelter from the cold outside, and had started a fire in a crock pot to warm themselves. The affected person was searched by the subject officer at the scene and again at a detachment. Some drug paraphernalia was confiscated. Asked whether they had consumed illicit substances in the last 24 hours, the affected person said they had not. The affected person was lodged in a cell. Later, upon returning to their cell from a phone room where they had contacted a lawyer, the affected person retrieved fentanyl from vials secreted in their groin area and consumed a quantity of the drug. They began to suffer the effects of the drug and became unresponsive on the cell floor. The subject officer entered the cell within minutes of the affected person’s medical event and attempted to rouse them with a sternum rub. They placed the affected person in the recovery position, administered a couple of doses of nasal naloxone and contacted EMS. Paramedics arrived on scene and took charge of the affected person’s care. They were transported to hospital and treated for acute fentanyl overdose.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police eyewitnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In the early afternoon of a day in early 2026, the affected person, while detained in a cell of a police detachment, reached into their underpants, retrieved a quantity of drugs and consumed them. About an hour later, they lapsed into medical distress with seizure-like symptoms. Custodial staff were quickly alerted to the affected person’s situation. They administered several doses of Narcan and summoned EMS. Paramedics arrived on scene and transported the affected person to hospital where they were treated for an overdose. The day before, acting on the strength of a warrant, two officers had arrested the affected person. One of the officers had conducted a pat-down search at the time, but failed to discover a body-pack around the affected person’s torso, which contained a plastic container of illicit substances. After being placed in a cruiser for transport to the detachment, the affected person was able to manipulate their handcuffed arms to retrieve the container from their pack and conceal it down the front of their underwear. At the detachment, the affected person revealed they were wearing the body-pack. It was removed from their possession and a quantity of methamphetamine was confiscated. A further pat-down search before the affected person was lodged in a cell failed to discover the container they were hiding.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and a subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, one of the subject officers did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, two subject officers were on-duty together assigned to a neighbourhood resource team, a proactive policing initiative by the service. The officers attended an apartment complex in the area to perform welfare checks, enforce conditions of release and execute outstanding arrest warrants. The complainant was identified as a person associated with the building who was subject to an arrest warrant on theft charges. The officers were just outside the elevator when it opened and the complainant stepped out. Asked to identify themselves, the complainant provided a false name and attempted to walk by the officers. The officers took hold of the complainant and a struggle ensued. The complainant was forcibly taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back. The complainant was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fractured left knee.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and both subject officers, gives rise to the following scenario. In the afternoon of early 2026, members of a divisional MCU were on the lookout for the affected person. They had information to believe that they had purchased a cellphone the day before with counterfeit money, and planned to arrest them for the offence. Operating unmarked vehicles and dressed in plainclothes, the officers located the affected person exiting an apartment building and tracked them to a grocery store, just around the corner from their residence in the area of a major street. They took up positions on the street outside the grocery store intending to take the affected person into custody on their exit from the store. Unaware they were being followed, the affected person left the store with a box of diapers in hand and started walking in the direction of their residence. They were confronted by an officer. The officer physically engaged the affected person and the two went to the ground by a stand of bicycle racks. Other members of the MCU arrived quickly at the site of the arrest, including another officer, and intervened. The second officer kneed the affected person twice in the left torso. The affected person was handcuffed behind the back and transported to the police station. The affected person complained of pain in the left arm and was transported to hospital where X-rays proved inconclusive because of swelling in the area. They were told to return to the fracture clinic for a follow-up assessment. Later, the affected person was X-rayed again in hospital and diagnosed with a radial head fracture of the left elbow.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police eyewitnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was his legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of his notes. In early 2026, police officers were on the lookout for a vehicle following a call to police from a person. The person had called to report that a family member – the affected person – had left their residence in the vehicle. They were of unsound mind and experiencing suicidal ideation. Officers located the vehicle and attempted to pull it over. The affected person refused to stop and picked up their speed. Multiple officers joined the pursuit and decided to attempt a rolling block of the vehicle. At one point, the officers positioned their cruisers around the vehicle and brought it to a stop. The officers exited their cruisers and attempted to engage with the affected person. The affected person had a knife that they occasionally held at their neck and abdomen. The officers realized that the affected person was only a risk to themselves, and put their firearms away. They were upset and asked the officers to kill them. They talked of being infected with spores and fungi. As time went by, the affected person became less responsive to the officers’ overtures. A team of tactical officers arrived on scene and surrounded the vehicle. Among their ranks was a trained negotiator who also attempted to bring the standoff to a peaceful resolution. The affected person could not be persuaded to let go of the knife and exit the vehicle. A plan was agreed that the tactical officers would storm the vehicle to take the affected person into custody when and if they placed the knife away from their person. At one point, the subject officer gave the signal to move in after the affected person had put the knife on the front passenger seat. Tactical officers smashed the front door windows and discharged less-lethal weapons at the affected person and spray into the vehicle. The affected person retrieved the knife and suffered a stab wound to the abdomen. The officers took possession of the knife and removed the affected person from the vehicle. The affected person was transported to hospital by paramedics and treated for a laceration to their abdomen.

summary_redacted: In the evening of early 2026, police officers were on the lookout for a vehicle being operated by someone who was possibly impaired. Police received information that a suspected impaired driver had left an area. About ten minutes later, the subject officer radioed that they had located the vehicle and were pursuing it. The driver, who was operating the vehicle, did not stop for the officer. Instead, they led the officer on a chase during which they disregarded traffic rules and travelled at high speeds. Having crossed a bridge, the driver continued at pace and lost control of the vehicle, slamming into a utility pole at an intersection. The driver was transported to hospital from the scene and diagnosed with injuries. The subject officer was a few car lengths away from the driver at the time of the collision. They had tracked the vehicle over a distance, at times at high speeds and through red lights without stopping.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and the subject officer, and other police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the early morning of a day in late 2025, an officer, on patrol in a marked vehicle, came across a pick-up truck stopped for a red light in Oakville. When the light turned green, the pick-up did not move. The officer exited their cruiser to check the driver and noticed them asleep with their head slumped forward. After banging on the window for a period, the officer was able to rouse the driver and directed them to pull over on the other side of the intersection. The driver was the affected person. With them in the front passenger seat was a female. The affected person proceeded south through the intersection, put on their four-way flashers as if they were going to stop, and then accelerated away. The officer radioed what had happened and began to pursue the truck. Other officers heard the transmissions and headed in the area of the pursuit to assist, including another officer in an unmarked police vehicle. The officer would come to occupy the lead cruiser in pursuit, in front of the first officer. The subject officer, also operating an unmarked police cruiser, intervened as well. The pursuit continued at speed for about 12 minutes. The affected person disregarded multiple red lights, as did police officers. A spike belt was deployed in front of the pick-up truck at one point, causing damage to the truck’s front driver side tire. As the pursuit turned onto a road in Mississauga, the three vehicles closest to the affected person’s truck – the officers – decided to perform a rolling block. In the area of the roadway’s intersection with another road, the subject officer overtook the truck and began to slow in front of it. The affected person brought the truck to an abrupt stop in front of the officer. Another officer stopped their cruiser alongside the driver side of the truck and a third officer directly behind. The subject officer exited their vehicle and approached the front of the first officer’s cruiser, pointing their semi-automatic pistol at the affected person in the driver’s seat of the truck. The officer was joined by the other officers, who each fired their CEWs at the affected person through the open driver’s door window. Orders were shouted at the affected person to exit the vehicle. The subject officer holstered their gun and took out their OC canister, which they sprayed at the affected person. Shortly after, the subject officer climbed on top of the hood of the first officer’s cruiser, grabbed a hold of the affected person’s leg, which had appeared through the driver’s window, and, with the other officers’ help, pulled the affected person out of the truck onto the hood. The affected person had their arms together by their chest and was on their right side on top of the hood when the subject officer punched them three times to the upper torso / head area. The affected person was pulled off the hood onto the ground in front of the first officer’s cruiser. There were now about six officers surrounding the affected person. Among them, the subject officer punched in the direction of the affected person’s torso five times and another officer dropped their knee onto the upper body and head area two or three times. The subject officer then stood up and kicked at the affected person’s right hip area six times. Shortly after, the affected person was handcuffed, stood up and lodged in the backseat of one of the cruisers. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with multiple facial fractures.
A person died from medical complications after surgery at a hospital. The investigation raises questions about post-operative care and clinical surveillance.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant, the subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. Shortly before midnight, in early 2026, police were asked to assist in the search of a suspect who had fled the scene of an attempted break and enter in Toronto and entered into the area with two accomplices in a vehicle. The vehicle had crashed on a street in the area, after which two of the suspects were arrested. The third took flight on foot and remained at large. Police deployed officers to the area of the collision and established a perimeter. Police dog teams were brought in to assist in the search, as was a police drone. The subject officers were part of the police operations. They were paired with a dog handler, who conducted a track of the suspect across residential streets in the area and the rear yards of homes. At about 1:40 a.m., with the assistance of residents in the neighbourhood sharing their surveillance camera footage with the police, the officers located a male – the complainant – hiding underneath a covering in the rear yard of a residence in the area. They alerted other officers, who arrived at the location to assist in taking the complainant into custody. The dog handler loudly announced that officers were present and directed the complainant to surrender. Initially, there was no response from the complainant. The officer warned that they would deploy their dog if the complainant did not surrender, at which point the complainant indicated they would come out. Another brief period passed before the officers moved in and physically engaged the complainant. They removed them from under the covering and dragged them a distance away. The complainant was struck several times by the officers, including in the face, before they were handcuffed behind the back. Following their arrest, the complainant was seen at hospital and diagnosed with several facial fractures and a broken finger.

A person died from a thoracic trauma after a collision between their bicycle and a vehicle in a Quebec municipality. They were riding their bicycle against traffic, without a helmet, intoxicated, and using a cell phone at the time of the accident.

Lors d'un exercice de tireurs actifs sur une base des Forces canadiennes, au début de 2024, un employé civil non participant aurait été détenu et fouillé violemment par des membres de la police militaire. Il aurait été contraint de s'allonger au sol, ses vêtements auraient été déchirés, ses effets personnels jetés par terre, et il aurait été soumis à un langage agressif incluant une référence à sa race. L'employé a subi un traumatisme important et a été en arrêt de travail pendant deux mois. La présidente de la Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a décidé, au milieu de 2025, de tenir une audience d'intérêt public, concluant que la plainte relève de la compétence de la Commission et soulevant des enjeux potentiels de brutalité policière et de profilage racial.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire — CPPM 2024-051

The Military Police Complaints Commission (MPCC) designated conduct complaint MPCC 2022-001 as a Public Interest Investigation (PII) under s. 250.38(1) of the National Defence Act. The complaint concerns allegations that the commanding officer and sergeant-major of a Military Police Unit gave preferential treatment to a Military Police Officer by failing to lay charges and covering up incidents of the officer's alcohol-related misconduct, including an attempted impaired driving incident and a child welfare concern. The complaint was joined to the existing PII covering MPCC files, which raised similar concerns about the same MPU leadership.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and the subject officer, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the late afternoon of late 2025, police officers were dispatched to an address in Hamilton, following a call to police about domestic abuse. A person reported that the affected person had threatened them earlier in the day and attended at their apartment where they caused a domestic disturbance. The subject officer, in the company of another officer, arrived at the address. They were aware that there were warrants in effect for the affected person’s arrest on firearm-related charges. A person spoke with the officers and indicated that the affected person was possibly at the rear of the residence. The subject officer walked to the side of the house and located the affected person sitting in a chair. They directed the affected person not to move, told them to stand up and then grabbed their arm, lifting them from the chair. The affected person immediately began to resist the officer. The two exchanged punches before falling to the ground where the struggle continued. The subject officer yelled out for the other officer’s help, and they appeared quickly and joined in the struggle. The affected person flailed their legs and refused to release their arms to be handcuffed. The subject officer and the other officer punched the affected person multiple times but could not sufficiently subdue them to take control of their arms behind the back. Another officer arrived on scene about two minutes after the altercation started and assisted in eventually handcuffing the affected person. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with injuries.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

summary_redacted: In the afternoon, a person was apprehended by the subject officer under the Mental Health Act. Police had been called to a location in Ottawa about a male behaving strangely. The officer arrived to find the person crying uncontrollably and muttering to himself. He made references to wanting to harm himself. The officer took the person into custody without incident. As they were cooperative, the officer did not feel the need to place them in handcuffs. The subject officer and the person arrived at a hospital, where they were joined by a witness officer. Another officer arrived and relieved the witness officer. The person became increasingly agitated and tried to leave the psychiatric evaluation section. Shortly after, the person confronted the officers and was warned that they would be handcuffed if they did not calm down. When their belligerence continued, the officers tried to control them and eventually forced them to the floor, resulting in an injury. The person was subsequently handcuffed and restrained to a hospital bed.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In late 2025, the subject officer and the witness officer were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a major road intersection in Kitchener, following calls to police from the resident, the affected person. The affected person threatened to kill herself if police did not attend her residence and indicated that she had cut her wrists. The officers arrived at the address, where they were joined by paramedics. The first responders made their way to the apartment door. Finding it locked, the subject officer kicked it open. The affected person was in her bedroom at the time. She was highly agitated and belligerent with the officers. A cut was visible on her wrist. The officers handcuffed the affected person behind the back and were escorting her down a hallway in the apartment when the witness officer briefly pinned her against a wall as the affected person’s hostility escalated. The affected person turned and spat on the witness officer, and was taken to the floor by the officers in a prone position. The affected person screamed at the officers to kill her and banged her face on the floor about a half-dozen times, fracturing her nose in the process. The subject officer took hold of the affected person’s head to prevent her hurting herself. The affected person was seen at hospital after her arrest and ultimately diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, the subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the afternoon of late 2025, the subject officer and their partner, another officer, were on the lookout for the affected person. The affected person was wanted for a number of break and enters, and subject to an outstanding arrest warrant. The officers located the affected person at a gas station in a rural area. The affected person was pumping gas into a vehicle. The officers pulled up in their unmarked vehicle, and exited. The subject officer approached the affected person and advised them they were under arrest. The affected person walked around the front of their vehicle away from the subject officer and towards the driver’s door. They confronted the other officer beside the door and pushed them out of the way, attempting to enter the vehicle. The other officer pushed back against the driver’s door to prevent it opening. The subject officer moved to assist. There followed a struggle in the course of which the subject officer used force against the affected person multiple times. With the assistance of civilians on scene, the officers overcame the affected person’s resistance and handcuffed them. The affected person was transported to hospital where diagnostic imaging was unable to rule out injuries to their ribs.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. Two subject officers travelled to a residence in the area to arrest the affected person for criminal harassment. They had earlier spoken to a woman, who reported that the affected person had stalked her and she was concerned for her safety, and the safety of her family. The affected person answered the officers’ knock on the door and was told they were to be arrested. They told the officers to get a warrant and closed the door, making their way to the basement. They called a family member, made remarks suggesting self-harm, and locked themselves in the basement bathroom where they proceeded to harm themselves. The subject officers were allowed into the residence by a witness, who expressed concern for the affected person’s safety. The officers went to the basement and started to speak to the affected person through the bathroom door. They told them they wanted to ensure their safety. The affected person said they were fine and taking a shower. The officers grew increasingly worried with the affected person’s wellbeing as their voice started to fade and attempted, unsuccessfully, to force open the door. Within minutes, additional officers arrived with a breach tool. The door was opened and officers entered. The affected person was inside the bathtub with injuries. Paramedics were called to the scene and transported them to hospital.

A person died from multiple injuries after falling from a structure in a city. The person had a pre-existing medical condition and had visited the emergency room several times before their death. The circumstances of their death raise questions about the handling of people in mental health crisis.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person made their way to a bridge. Their intention was to jump from the bridge. They called 911 to advise emergency responders of their intention and provided them with their location. Two call-takers – one from a police service and another from the ambulance service – spoke to the affected person. They assured them they had done the right thing by calling them, asked them not to jump from the bridge, and told them help was on its way. The affected person stayed on the line for several minutes. At the very end, they told the police call-taker that they could hear and see responding police officers, and that they were going to jump. They proceeded to do just that. The subject officer was the first officer on scene. They stopped their cruiser in a parking lot by the bridge, exited and was making their way to the affected person’s position when the affected person jumped from the bridge onto some snow-covered rocks below. The officer provided care and comfort to the affected person while they waited for paramedics. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with fractures of the feet and knee.

A person died in hospital in a region due to a cardiac arrhythmia caused by an acute myocardial infarction. The death occurred in a context of severe coronary disease, complex medical history, and recent substance use. A visit to the emergency room the previous day did not diagnose the infarction, with symptoms attributed to a pre-existing condition.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may be briefly summarized. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person was with her boyfriend, an off-duty officer, on a street in Toronto when the two quarreled and she left the area telling him that she was going to jump. She made her way to a bridge over a river and called the officer using a passerby’s cellphone. She told him she was going to commit suicide and that it would be his fault. The officer called police to report that the affected person was in mental health crisis and threatening to harm herself. He would subsequently learn from a third-party whose phone the affected person had used to call him that she was in the area of the bridge and a roadway. The subject officer drove in that direction, travelling across the bridge. With him was another officer. The subject officer approached the side of the bridge, brought his vehicle to a stop and exited with the other officer. They were just out of their cruiser when the affected person scaled the bridge guardrail and jumped down the other side. She landed on the frozen river below, a distance of about eight metres. The two officers made their way to the riverbank and walked onto the river to render aid to the affected person. Firefighters attended the scene and assisted in her removal. She was transported to hospital and diagnosed with fractures and injuries to her internal organs.

summary_redacted_fr: Un étudiant en chimie à l'Université McGill est décédé des suites d'une intoxication au cyanure à Montréal. L'étudiant faisait face à une plainte disciplinaire et était exclu du campus, mais continuait à poursuivre sa thèse à distance. Malgré le soutien psychologique disponible, il n'y a pas eu de suivi actif. Les circonstances entourant le décès soulèvent des inquiétudes quant à la gestion de l'accès aux substances toxiques dans un environnement universitaire et à la prévention du suicide dans les milieux académiques.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the morning of early 2026, officers from the Ontario Provincial Police were looking for a SUV involved in several collisions in the Rockland area. The vehicle was being operated in a reckless manner and had significant damage to the front passenger side. An officer spotted the SUV and performed a U-turn to follow it, activating emergency lights and siren. The SUV pulled over and stopped, and the officer approached the driver, who was alone in the vehicle. The driver was told they were being detained for a "hit and run" but refused to exit the vehicle and began to grapple with the officer. Another officer arrived on scene and found the two fighting in the road. This officer threw several punches in the direction of the driver's head, and the driver was eventually handcuffed and arrested. Following the arrest, the driver was seen at hospital and diagnosed with a fractured right orbital bone.

summary_redacted: In the afternoon, a person was apprehended by the subject officer under the Mental Health Act. Police had been called to a location in Ottawa about a male behaving strangely. The officer arrived to find the person crying uncontrollably and muttering to himself, making references to wanting to harm himself. The officer took the person into custody without incident. At the hospital, the person became increasingly agitated and tried to leave. After being warned to calm down, the person continued to be belligerent, and the officers attempted to control them. The person struggled, and the officers tried to force them to the floor, resulting in the person falling and fracturing their nose. The person was subsequently restrained.

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted_fr: La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a tenu une audience d'intérêt public portant sur des plaintes déposées par la famille d'un membre des Forces canadiennes décédé par suicide. Les enquêtes menées par le Service national des enquêtes des Forces canadiennes en 2008, 2009 et 2010 ont été examinées, notamment la gestion de la scène de décès, la non-divulgation de la note de suicide à la famille, les lacunes professionnelles et d'indépendance des enquêteurs, ainsi que les interactions du Service national des enquêtes avec les plaignants. La Commission a formulé de nombreuses conclusions et recommandations à l'intention du Grand Prévôt des Forces canadiennes.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire (CPPM) — dossier CPPM 2011-004

summary_redacted: A complaint was filed with the Military Police Complaints Commission in early 2007 alleging that Military Police failed to investigate possible detainee abuse in Afghanistan. Three detainees captured in a region in 2006 showed signs of physical injury upon transfer to an airfield, yet Military Police allegedly failed to conduct medical examinations, failed to investigate the cause of the injuries, failed to properly seize and inventory personal effects, and transferred the detainees to local police without completing standard procedures. The complaint, filed under a section of the National Defence Act, requested a public interest investigation and hearing under another section.
tribunal_body: Military Police Complaints Commission — Afghanistan Public Interest Investigation

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was his legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. He did authorize the release of his notes. In early 2026, two officers were dispatched to a location in Toronto following a 911 call. The caller did not address the call-taker but utterances were heard in the background suggesting an ongoing and potentially violent disturbance involving a male and female. The officers entered the home despite objections from the woman who opened the door – the affected person. The affected person was placed under arrest by one of the officers after he observed a male – the affected person – with a cut to the forehead. The affected person refused to cooperate as the officer sought to question him about what had happened. Standing on a second floor landing, the affected person asked the officers to leave and then called 911 asking for additional officers. The officers told the affected person to disconnect the 911 call as they were the police. When he failed to do so, they warned that he would be arrested for public mischief. Shortly after, the officers took hold of the affected person to take him into custody. The affected person refused to surrender his arms to be handcuffed and was grounded. On the floor, he resisted the officers’ efforts to control his arms behind the back and was met with a punch to the face by one of the officers. Moments later, as the struggle continued, the officer delivered two punches to the affected person’s head in quick succession. It would take another five minutes, during which the other officer kicked the affected person once or twice in the legs and exhorted him verbally to give up his arms, before the officers wrestled control of his arms behind the back and secured them in handcuffs. The affected person was transported to hospital after his arrest and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and additional police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a location in response to a report of a person attempting to break into a residence. Two officers were the first on scene, arriving at about 3:10 a.m. They were joined shortly by a police dog handler, the subject officer, and their dog. Additional officers arrived on scene and set up a perimeter around the house as the subject officer deployed their dog to locate the suspect. It was not long before the dog led the subject officer to a crawl space underneath a deck. The affected person was hiding in the crawl space, which was cluttered with stored items. They failed to come out and give any indication of their presence despite repeated direction from the subject officer for them to do so. With the dog still attached to the lead, the subject officer released the dog into the crawl space. The dog’s first foray into the space was unsuccessful. The subject officer released the dog into the crawl space again. On this occasion, the dog located the affected person under some items and bit into their left arm. The affected person gave no reaction. The subject officer approached their dog and the affected person and released the dog’s hold, after which the affected person was taken into custody. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture and dog bites to the left arm.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and both subject officers, gives rise to the following scenario. In the afternoon of early 2026, members of a divisional MCU were on the lookout for the affected person. They had information to believe that they had purchased a cellphone the day before with counterfeit money, and planned to arrest them for the offence. Operating unmarked vehicles and dressed in plainclothes, the officers located the affected person exiting an apartment building and tracked them to a grocery store, just around the corner from their residence in the area of a major street. They took up positions on the street outside the grocery store intending to take the affected person into custody on their exit from the store. Unaware they were being followed, the affected person left the store with a box of diapers in hand and started walking in the direction of their residence. They were confronted by an officer. The officer physically engaged the affected person and the two went to the ground by a stand of bicycle racks. Other members of the MCU arrived quickly at the site of the arrest, including another officer, and intervened. The officer kneed the affected person twice in the left torso. The affected person was handcuffed behind the back and transported to the police station. The affected person complained of pain in the left arm and was transported to hospital where X-rays proved inconclusive because of swelling in the area. They were told to return to the fracture clinic for a follow-up assessment. Later, the affected person was X-rayed again in hospital and diagnosed with a radial head fracture of the left elbow.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a city. The person who called 911 wanted the affected person removed from their residence. The subject officer arrived on scene with another officer shortly before 11:00 p.m., joined shortly by a third officer. The person who called 911 met them outside the building and provided them a key to the apartment. One of the officers opened the door, stepped inside and was splashed with cooking oil from a bottle by the affected person. The affected person retreated into the kitchen area and was physically engaged by the officers. They were taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back by the subject officer. The affected person was placed in the backseat of the subject officer's cruiser and transported to a police detention facility. En route, they slipped the handcuffs under their legs so that they were then positioned in front of their body. In statements made to police personnel at the station, they would explain that the pain in their left wrist was the result of this maneuver. The next day, the affected person was seen at a hospital and diagnosed with a fractured left wrist.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted_fr: La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a mené une enquête d'intérêt public sur la conduite de la police militaire dans une affaire d'agression sexuelle. L'enquête a révélé des lacunes dans l'enquête, notamment l'absence d'enregistrement de l'entrevue avec la personne affectée et la présence d'un témoin potentiel dans la salle d'interrogatoire. La Commission a formulé des recommandations pour améliorer les enquêtes sur les allégations d'agression sexuelle.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire (CPPM)

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In early 2025, police were called to a residence in Brantford. A resident of the building had called police to report a disturbance coming from another unit. Two officers attended at the scene and knocked at the door. The disturbance involved the affected person and another individual. The affected person was subject to an order at the time preventing contact and communication with the other individual. Realizing police were outside the door and that they would be arrested for breaching the order, the affected person attempted to escape apprehension. They climbed through a window of their unit onto the roof of an adjoining structure, ran across the roof to an exterior staircase and started to descend. Two other officers had also responded to the disturbance call and were outside as the first two officers entered. They observed the affected person running on the roof and were waiting for them at the bottom of the staircase. When the affected person jumped over the staircase railing at the midway landing and collapsed on the ground below, the officers approached to take them into custody. The affected person complained of pain to their foot and was transported to hospital where they were diagnosed with a broken ankle.

summary_redacted_fr: Une personne dans une situation vulnérable est décédée d'une arrestation cardiorespiratoire à l'hôpital de Val-d'Or.
