finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted:
The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. The subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes, as was their legal right.

In the early afternoon of Q4 2025, Toronto Police Service (TPS) officers were called to a hotel located in the Yonge Street area of Toronto. Hotel staff had contacted police to seek the removal of a guest — the complainant. The complainant had caused a commotion in the lobby and accused hotel employees of being associated with persons seeking her death. The complainant was of unsound mind at the time of her interaction with the officers. Since checking into the hotel a few days earlier, the complainant had frequently attended the front desk to complain about persons wanting her dead. She had returned to her room by the time of the officers' arrival.

A group of TPS officers arrived on scene and, accompanied by the hotel manager, took the elevator to the 18th floor and attended at the complainant's room. Among them was the subject officer. The subject officer knocked on the door and asked for the complainant. The complainant indicated she had not called police and refused to open the door. The subject officer indicated that the officers would open the door if she did not let them in. The complainant did not respond. A female officer interjected and attempted to speak with the complainant, assuring her she was not in trouble and that they simply wanted to talk. Still, there was no response. Officers attempted to open the door, including with a master card key, but were unsuccessful as it had been double-locked from the inside. A TPS Mobile Crisis Intervention Team (MCIT) arrived on scene and also tried to have the complainant open the door.

On learning from hotel staff that the complainant's room had a Juliet balcony from which the complainant could jump, a witness officer entered an adjacent room and onto its balcony. From that vantage point, the officer observed that the door to the Juliet balcony was open. Looking down, the officer observed the complainant lying on the roof of the third floor and immediately radioed for paramedics. The roof was accessed by Toronto Fire Service. The complainant was pronounced deceased shortly thereafter.

At autopsy, the pathologist was of the preliminary view that the complainant's death was attributable to multiple blunt force trauma.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2026, the subject officer, in the company of two other officers, attended at an apartment in Oshawa. A neighbouring tenant had called 911 to report the sounds of a woman screaming coming from the apartment. Officers knocked on the door and asked the woman to open it. She tried but was unable to open the door. She could be heard moaning in distress and saying that she had been choked. One of the officers retrieved a ram and the subject officer used it to break open the door. The woman was by the doorway and pointed in the direction of a hallway. The affected person appeared in the hallway. He was at the apartment in violation of a bail order. One of the officers ordered the affected person to put his hands behind his back. The affected person refused to do so and instead walked towards the officer. The officer grabbed a hold of the affected person and attempted to wrestle control of his arms behind his back. The affected person refused to release his arms. There followed a struggle during which the affected person was forced to the hallway floor and subjected to multiple punches by the officers, and the use of a CEW, before he was handcuffed behind his back. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to hospital on two occasions before he was eventually diagnosed with a broken rib.

finding: unsubstantiated
summary_redacted_fr: La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a mené une enquête d'intérêt public à la suite d'une plainte alléguant que des policiers militaires avaient maltraité des détenus à l'étranger lors d'un exercice d'entraînement, et que le commandant sur place avait ignoré les accusations qui lui avaient été soumises. Après examen de documents et des entrevues avec des témoins, la Commission a conclu que les allégations n'étaient pas vérifiées et qu'aucune preuve de mauvais traitement des détenus n'avait été découverte. La Commission a néanmoins formulé des recommandations visant à améliorer les pratiques d'enquête de la police militaire, notamment en matière de prise de notes, de préparation des entrevues et de gestion des dossiers après une opération à l'étranger.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire — EIP 2015-005

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the subject officer and another police eyewitness, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. In the evening of late 2025, police officers, including the subject officer, were dispatched to a multi-unit address in a town. A resident of the address had contacted police to report concern with the wellbeing of a male – the affected person – causing a disturbance at the property. He had been talking to himself and threatening to kill someone. The subject officer arrived on scene, joined by other officers. They attended at a unit of the building and arrested the affected person on a warrant in effect for their arrest for breach of a probation order. They were handcuffed behind the back, escorted outside and searched by the officers. A number of items were seized. The affected person was subsequently placed in the backseat of a police cruiser and read their rights by an officer. An officer exited the cruiser, leaving the affected person alone. They and another officer returned to the unit to search the residence for firearms. Left alone in the cruiser, the affected person slipped their handcuffed arms below their legs and repositioned them to the front. They subsequently reached towards the crotch area of their pants, retrieved a bag and ingested a white substance it contained. Shortly after the affected person’s consumption of the substance, the subject officer began to monitor them from outside the cruiser using the lights from their cruiser and a flashlight. The affected person brought their hands up to their mouth area on several occasions. Having concluded the search of the unit, an officer returned to their cruiser and checked on the affected person in the rear. The affected person was shaking and pale. Noting that the handcuffs were now to the front, and suspecting a drug overdose, the officer requested EMS. The affected person was removed from the cruiser, administered two doses of Narcan and placed in the recovery position pending the arrival of paramedics. EMS arrived and the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for drug overdose.

A person died due to obstruction of the airways caused by food choking in a hospital. The circumstances surrounding the death raise concerns about the quality of care and services provided, particularly with regard to the management of special diets and the communication of critical information between care teams.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In early 2026, the subject officer sat stationary in their marked cruiser in a parking lot at the southeast corner of a city intersection. They and their passenger – a witness officer – were manning a perimeter point at that location, their cruiser stopped facing the roadway. At about 2:43 a.m., two vehicles travelled past their location at excessive speed – a white vehicle and a black vehicle - as if they were racing each other. The subject officer pulled onto the roadway and accelerated after the vehicles, intending to stop them in connection with their speed. The driver of the black vehicle was driving with several passengers, including the affected person in the backseat. The vehicles continued at speeds as high as 170 km/h. Approaching an intersection – approximately a kilometre-and-a-half from the starting location, the driver came upon traffic stopped at a red light. They applied their brakes but crashed into two of the vehicles – a transport truck and another vehicle. The affected person suffered a fractured hip in the collision. The subject officer arrived on scene and arrested the driver for dangerous driving.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the complainant and police witnesses, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police officers were looking for the complainant. They had left the scene of a domestic disturbance threatening suicide and the police were concerned for their wellbeing. A “ping” of the cell phone in the complainant’s possession returned a location in the area of a city. The subject officer, in the company of two witness officers, arrived in the area. There was no answer at the first house they approached but one of the witness officers had noticed a light on at the house next door. The complainant had fled the scene of the disturbance and taken refuge in an empty house in the area. When one of the witness officers initially approached the house and asked them to identify themselves, the complainant provided a false name. With the officer now off the property, the complainant made their way outside to the rear of the property and inside a steel shed. They had a knife in their possession. Not long after the witness officer had left the residence where they encountered the complainant, the officer learned that, in fact, they had been dealing with the complainant. They, the subject officer and the other witness officer returned to the residence and eventually located the complainant in the rear shed. The complainant displayed a knife and began to walk with purpose towards the officers, repeatedly telling them to shoot them. The subject officer and one of the witness officers walked away from the shed and ordered the complainant to drop the knife. They did so, throwing it away, and continued their advance. When they were within striking range of the subject officer, the officer punched them in the face. The complainant was felled by the blow but continued to resist on the ground by refusing to surrender their arms. The witness officers delivered several more strikes to the torso and legs, after which the complainant was handcuffed behind the back. The complainant was diagnosed at hospital after their arrest with a fractured orbital bone.

The agency's final report regarding a shooting death in a rural area of British Columbia was released. The incident involved a person who shot out the tires of a vehicle containing their family members, who were on their way to an event in another town. The driver of the vehicle was able to proceed to a nearby residence, but the person followed them and shot out the remaining tires. The person eventually returned to their residence after several failed attempts to speak with their family. The family travelled on to the event and remained out of town until the matter could be resolved, on the advice of the police. Attempts were made by police officers to speak with the person, but they were not cooperative. The police agency's Emergency Response Team was ultimately activated. The incident was investigated under the Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act, Subsection 45.72(2).
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2009-2863 — Commission's Final Report after Commissioner's Response Regarding the Shooting Death of Valeri George in Buick Creek, B.C.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, a subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, a subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of late 2025, police officers, including two subject officers, attended an apartment in the area of a city. The resident – the affected person had contacted police to report that an inebriated person was in her apartment and she wanted them removed. The officers entered the apartment and approached the person in the living room. They were seated on a sofa and speaking on the phone with a police call-taker. After a period, the person ended their conversation on the phone, stood up from the sofa and walked a short distance towards the door. Just before the door, the officers took a hold of their arms. The person attempted to free their arms and the officers maintained their grip. Other officers intervened and the person fell forward to the floor, striking their head off the edge of a wall in the process. Officers fell with the person and landed on top of them. They were handcuffed in quick order and taken into custody. Following their arrest, the person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a reduced level of consciousness and a scalp laceration with emphysema and subgaleal hematoma.

A person died from septic shock secondary to a pleural empyema in a city after undergoing cardiac surgery. Despite clinical signs of infection during post-operative follow-ups, no complementary examination was performed.

The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) is responsible for conducting investigations into all officer-related incidents which result in death or "serious harm" within the province of British Columbia. As the Chief Civilian Director of the IIO, they are required to review all investigations upon their conclusion, in order to determine whether they consider that an officer may have committed an offence under any enactment. If they conclude that an officer may have committed an offence, they are required to report the matter to Crown Counsel. If they do not make a report to Crown Counsel, they are permitted to publicly report the reasoning underlying their decision. This is a public report related to the investigation into the injury of an adult male that occurred in the city of Vancouver. The affected person sustained serious injuries after being shot in the hand with a rubber bullet by a member of the Emergency Response Team of the police service.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In the early morning of 2026, the affected person was driving in Brampton when a police cruiser started to follow their vehicle, attempting to pull them over. The affected person did not stop for the cruiser. They turned into some side streets, ultimately losing control of their vehicle because of icy road conditions and striking parked cars. The affected person exited their car and fled the scene on foot through some backyards. An officer was on patrol when their ALPR registered a hit on the affected person's vehicle, indicating that the registered owner was a suspended driver. The officer briefly chased the affected person on foot and broadcast that they were subject to arrest for failing to remain at the scene of an accident. The affected person made their way home to the area, about six kilometres east of the collision scene. The subject officer was waiting for them in the area and grabbed a hold of the affected person, throwing them to the ground, just as they were attempting to enter the house. The officer kept the affected person pinned to the ground with their body weight for a period before they were joined by another officer, responding to the scene. The affected person struggled against the officers' efforts to control their arms and bit an officer's finger. The subject officer kneed the affected person in the left ribs and pulled them onto their left side. The parties continued to wrestle for control of the affected person's arms and the subject officer delivered another knee strike to the torso. With the arrival and intervention of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed to the back and taken into custody. The affected person was charged with multiple offences and released at the scene. They subsequently attended hospital and were diagnosed with a fractured left rib.

L'employé n'aurait pas participé à l'exercice et n'aurait pas consenti à l'intervention; il aurait subi un traitement agressif incluant l'usage excessif de la force, des propos violents, et une fouille de ses effets personnels. La présidente de la Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a décidé, au milieu de 2025, de tenir une audience d'intérêt public, invoquant la gravité des allégations, les enjeux systémiques possibles et l'intérêt public.

summary_redacted: In the afternoon, in Stratford, the subject officer observed the affected person in a public area. Aware that the affected person was subject to a warrant for their arrest, the officer contacted uniformed personnel to respond to the area to take the affected person into custody. A witness officer arrived in a fully marked cruiser. The affected person ran and the subject officer exited their vehicle, ran across the street, and tackled the affected person to the ground. The affected person attempted to get up and was held down by the subject officer and the witness officer. A struggle ensued during which the subject officer delivered several punches to the head and torso of the affected person, and a knee strike to their side. With the assistance of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed. Following the arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a serious injury.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the early afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer was dispatched to a gas station following a call to police about a male kicking a door at the premises. The subject officer arrived on scene, parked their cruiser in the parking lot, and located the male – the affected person. They detained the affected person and subsequently arrested them on the strength of several outstanding warrants. The subject officer handcuffed the affected person behind the back and conducted a pat-down search by the side of the cruiser, confiscating several items, after which they placed them in the rear of their vehicle. Another officer arrived on scene in their cruiser to take custody of the affected person and transport them to the station. They and the subject officer approached the rear of the subject officer’s cruiser, opened the passenger door, and observed that the affected person had brought their handcuffed hands to the front and their shoes were off. The officers removed the affected person and subjected them to a secondary search. On this occasion, they located substances in the inner pockets of the affected person’s coat. Following the search, en route to the station, the officer observed that something was in the affected person’s mouth. They said that they had consumed a substance, at which point the officer pulled over and called for an ambulance. While waiting for the ambulance, the officer pleaded with the affected person to spit out what was in their mouth. Other officers arrived on scene and did the same. The affected person said that they would rather die than go to police cells. Paramedics attended and transported the affected person to hospital. They were unresponsive with persistent bradycardia on arrival. They were monitored overnight and released into the custody of the police the following day.

summary_redacted: The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) is responsible for conducting investigations into all officer-related incidents which result in death or "serious harm" within the province of British Columbia. As the Chief Civilian Director of the IIO, they are required to review all investigations upon their conclusion. If they conclude that an officer may have committed an offence, they are required to report the matter to Crown counsel. This is a supplemental public report related to the investigation into the injury of an adult female that occurred in a city in British Columbia. In the first public report, it was indicated that IIO investigators had interviewed civilian witnesses to this incident.
tribunal_body: IIO file 2013-000040 — off duty RCMP officer

Des policiers militaires ont effectué une visite de bien-être auprès d'une ancienne membre de la police militaire dont les enfants avaient été laissés sans surveillance. Des supérieurs de l'unité de police militaire auraient ordonné une « pause tactique » dans l'enquête et contacté la famille de la personne visée pour l'informer de la visite. Un policier militaire ayant refusé d'interrompre l'enquête a fait l'objet d'une enquête disciplinaire d'unité pour insubordination, alléguant des représailles en lien avec sa plainte antérieure. La CPPM a décidé de mener une enquête d'intérêt public sur cette plainte pour ingérence.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire du Canada (CPPM)

summary_redacted: The incident occurred in Chilliwack, B.C. on November 19, 2007. A hit and run incident happened on Yale Road at Airport Road. The affected person arrived at a rental centre to return a wood chipper. A couple reported the hit and run to the RCMP Detachment. A witness spoke to a store owner about the hit and run driver, and the conversation was overheard by the affected person. The affected person paid for their rental and called their father. The store owner physically prevented the affected person from going upstairs and instructed an employee to call the police. The employee called the RCMP to report a disturbance. The RCMP was called back to confirm if police presence was still needed, and assistance was confirmed. The RCMP dispatched a request for members to attend.
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2007-2427 — Appendices: Chair's Interim Report -  Public Interest Investigation into the   In-Custody Death of Robert Thurston Knipstrom

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, the affected person was at a public square to protest against a demonstration by a movement. The demonstrators had made their way to the public square from another location, where they had initially gathered. The affected person, with a group of other people, had also been at the other location attempting to disrupt the demonstration. Police officers had been assigned to police the demonstration and counter-protest. They were to preserve the peace and attempt to keep the rival factions separated. At the public square, officers used their bicycles to erect a perimeter between the demonstrators, at the centre of the square, and the counter-protesters, on its periphery. The affected person was in the area of the northeast corner of the square when they and others were asked to leave the square. The affected person began to slowly walk away but stopped to object to the treatment of a person, who was being physically escorted by police in the direction of a roadway. The subject officer ordered them to leave or they would be arrested for obstructing police. The officer then pushed the affected person by the arms northward towards the roadway, the roadway that bordered the square to the north. The affected person told the subject officer that they did not have to touch them and turned to face the officer. The subject officer reacted by turning them around and pushing them in the back towards the roadway. The affected person stumbled forward and fell onto the sidewalk, fracturing their hip in the process. The affected person was transported by paramedics to hospital and treated for their injury.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police eyewitness, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, the subject officer and another officer were dispatched to a convenience store in a area. A store employee had contacted police to report a male and female shoplifter. The officers arrived on scene and spoke with store staff, confirming that two suspects had left the store with alcohol and food without paying for the items. The employee showed a video clip they had taken with their cellphone, depicting the male and female. The officers located the suspects – a person and the affected person – at a nearby bus shelter. One person was taken into custody by an officer without incident. The affected person, however, resisted when the subject officer attempted to secure them in handcuffs. The officer was able to affix a cuff to the right hand but had difficulty attaching the other cuff to the affected person’s left hand. The affected person objected to their arrest, denied that they had done anything wrong, and struggled against the subject officer’s efforts to bring their arms behind the back. The tussle eventually made its way outside the bus shelter where another officer intervened. The officers brought the affected person to the ground, after which they secured both their hands in handcuffs. Following their arrest, the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for a fractured right wrist.

finding: grounds_found
summary_redacted: The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) conducted an investigation into an officer-related incident that resulted in serious harm to an adult female in the city of Vancouver. The affected person, an innocent bystander, sustained a serious injury while an adult male was being taken into custody. The Chief Civilian Director of the IIO reviewed the investigation and determined whether an officer may have committed an offence. Pursuant to the Police Act, the CCD is required to report the matter to Crown Counsel if it is considered that an officer may have committed an offence. This public report summarizes the circumstances and results of the investigation.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may briefly be summarized. In early 2026, the subject officer sat stationary in their marked cruiser in a parking lot at the southeast corner of a major intersection. They and their passenger – a witness officer – were manning a perimeter point at that location, their cruiser stopped facing the roadway. At about 2:43 a.m., two vehicles travelled past their location at excessive speed – a white vehicle and a black vehicle - as if they were racing each other. The subject officer pulled onto the roadway and accelerated after the vehicles, intending to stop them in connection with their speed. The driver of the black vehicle was driving with several passengers, including the affected person, in the backseat. The vehicles continued at speeds as high as 170 km/h. Approaching a major road – approximately a kilometre-and-a-half from the starting location, the driver came upon traffic stopped at a red light. They applied their brakes but crashed into two of the vehicles – a transport truck and another vehicle. The affected person suffered a fractured hip in the collision. The subject officer arrived on scene and arrested the driver for dangerous driving.
A decision was made in the matter of a shooting involving the RCMP in Grande Prairie in late 2025.
tribunal_body: PRC AB — ASIRT file 2025-0048(N) — RCMP — decision: https://albertaprc.ca/media/o23faprv/act-ed-dec-rcmp-shooting-in-grande-prairie-20260409.pdf

summary_redacted: A fatal fire occurred in an apartment in a region, resulting in the death of the affected person. The fire started in the bedroom, likely due to a smoking-related incident, and the smoke alarm was not functioning. The coroner's office is recommending systematic inspections of buildings after fatal fires to ensure compliance with safety regulations.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP
